text: Like many have already said, this looks like a jugaad job till the Born EV platform is ready. I really hope the final product is finished well, but I honestly don't see too much effort going in here with all their focus on the Born EV's that will come by 2024/25.

So many missing features No regenerative adjustment No fog lamps No wireless charger Dashboard design from 90's 7"inch HU looks outdated Not even a lamp in glove box No auto dimming IRVM No rear AC vents No auto AC No dual zone climate control A suggestion to Mahindra, please remove the useless sunroof and add above features in the final product please. Quote: Originally Posted by fiat_tarun Like many have already said, this looks like a jugaad job till the Born EV platform is ready. Why is this a Jugaad job? Then Nexon Tigor and Tiago all become JUGAADS. Even the MG ZS also becomes a jugaad. ICE vehicle engineered to an EV is a proper Engineering job in my opinion and not a jugaad.

Extremely disappointed with Mahindra's approach with their first long range EV. I had seen Mahindra showcase their E-XUV300 (as it was called then) in the Auto Expo 2022 at Delhi. From then on I was eagerly waiting for its launch, knowing that XUV300 has a much better chassis compared to Nexon and also comes with 6 airbags, which I feel is a miss in Nexon EV. Dont mistake me, Nexon is a very good chassis in itself. But in comparative terms, I feel XUV300 to be better owing to its higher width and longer wheelbase, minus the boot space. I was very excited when Mahindra revealed that they have increased the total length of the car to 4.2m, which would finally solve XUV300's major issue, the boot. I was considering this as a replacement for our Ford global Fiesta 2012 DCT, to be used by my father. All hopes fell, when Mahindra launched a half baked car without even revealing the prices . Upon that there has been complete radio silence since the launch from Mahindra. Interior is not a problem for me. Even though its old in terms of styling, its not a deal breaker considering the other good things of the car. The other things which I dint like were that Mahindra has used an older generation NMC cells instead of the proven LFP chemistry. Also adjustable regen is a major miss. Considering that the tax exemptions for EVs under 80EEB is going to end in March 2023, we wanted to have our purchase before that. Seeing the existing load on Mahindra's plants, I doubt Mahindra would start its delivery of XUV400 within that. Also considering its a 1st generation long range EV product from Mahindra, I was skeptical to have it as my father's primary car. Upon that the half baked demo cars at launch did not do any good. Thus have booked Nexon EV Max and awaiting delivery. Being an EV enthusiast from a long time, I had high hopes when Mahindra purchased Reva. Was hoping they will lead the EV way in India with much better products. On the contrary, they seem to have gone the other way. They kept delaying their launches and the after sales service of their E20 series has been below average. Tata came completely out of syllabus in this manner and kudos to them. . Hope Mahindra correct their mistake with the Born Electric range of EVs.

Upon that there has been complete radio silence Frankly, why is XUV400 a half baked car? At the media drives, I think Mahindra made their roadmap fully clear regarding the launch of the XUV400. And not a single reviewer called it a half baked product. Why is NMC called an old technology as compared to LFP? Nickel-manganese-cobalt (NMC) batteries are the most common form found in EVs today, ranging from the Nissan Leaf to Mercedes-Benz EQS . Nissan is pioneer in EVs in the world and Mercedes in Indian minds is the supremo of auto engineering. Who knows Mahindra may offer it via a software update later just as TATA has done with the TIGOR which is getting new features via software update. I would wait for the vehicle to come to showrooms and take a test drive. Lets give an Indian manufacturer manufacturing and engineering and designing in India a chance just as we are rejoicing an Indian origin person becoming PM of a country.

About the lack of adjustable regen. It seems to me that the most intuitive reflex action for braking is stomping on the brake pedal, for most drivers. A person with a care free attitude would prefer everything to be intuitive And it can be, if upon pressing the brake pedal, the regen gets progressively more powerful until, at the point where the car has to stop, the actual brake pads make physical contact along with the maximum regen setting It seems that this just could be the case

Quote: Originally Posted by sagsaw Why is NMC called an old technology as compared to LFP? Nickel-manganese-cobalt (NMC) batteries are the most common form found in EVs today, ranging from the Nissan Leaf to Mercedes-Benz EQS . Nissan is pioneer in EVs in the world and Mercedes in Indian minds is the supremo of auto engineering. Few reasons, why more manufacturers are switching to LFP: LFP typically has 2 to 3 times more recharge cycles for same level of battery degradation, ~1000/1500 for NMC vs ~3000 for LFP for 20% degradation. Especially helps with mid/small batteries i.e. less than 50 kwh, which will go through recharge cycles faster. LFP has better high temperature tolerance, so good for tropical countries like India. Note that Nissan Leaf is typically sold in western countries with cooler climate. LFP has better fire safety Lesser dependence on rare/conflict materials like Cobalt It's main disadvantage is lesser energy density i.e. it will weigh more and take more space as compared to NMC, but this can be taken care in the car design as shown by Tata, MG, BYD, Tesla and many more who are switching to LFP The other reason why it feels half-baked is the very dated cabin looks and controls, XUV 300 itself feels overdue for a facelift. Now you have a new car hopefully available in 2023 with ~5 year old looks. Quote: Originally Posted by sagsaw Adjustable regen modes might not be there but without the test drive how will you know the tuning of the regen? The main reason for adjustable regen is no single setting is optimal  across driving conditions, you want max regen in city for single pedal  bumper to bumper driving, some where in between for moderate traffic and  no regen for highway driving. Maybe they can tune it with drive modes,  but ideally you want independent control over regen. Quote: Originally Posted by sagsaw Who knows Mahindra may offer it via a software update later just as TATA has done with the TIGOR which is getting new features via software update. The software update route worked out for Nexon, because it had MID controls on the steering. Hope the XUV400 has similar controls for easy access. I think Tata has not yet rolled it out for Tigor and it is going to be difficult to make it easily accessible without steering controls. If you hear what Anand Kulkarni of Tata Motors said, its clear Tigor is getting updates in a phased manner very soon. He said two phases of updates. India is still not even Gen 1 with respect to EVs, so about Regen modes and it's optimal usability all will take more uears and EV miles to have that data and then optimize with respect to that data of practical cuatomer usage patterns. So its pure theoretical whether auto regen is optimal or modes are optimal etc. Thats my opinion though. Using NMC doesnt mean old technology or unsafe one. Time will tell. MnM chose one option Tata chose the other. Cooling systems play a big role and at 400 reveal, MnM explained in detail how they have safety first approach with respect to the battery pack. BMS and Charging modules too play a big part in battery overall health. Nexon too is an old product. Hasnt changed much excepts some touches here and there. As I said you like a cabin or you dont but that doesnt imply half baked. ICE to Electric doesnt naturally imply that cabin has to change. MnM has their own pattern of product launches and 400 is no different. So lets wait for January 2023 price announcement. Overall lets celebrate an Indian manufacturer (anyone and everyone including OLA) making in India and save the ifs n buts after the product goes on sale.

Quote: Originally Posted by sagsaw Why is NMC called an old technology as compared to LFP? I never said that NMC is an older technology. I said that the cells that Mahindra is sourcing from LG is of an older generation. Below is an extract from the Autocar India review of the XUV400 which mentions that. Quote: The NMC battery pack, sourced from LG Chem in Korea, is an older-generation chemistry (5.3.2) and Mahindra engineers have had to balance power and range Quote: Originally Posted by sagsaw Nickel-manganese-cobalt (NMC) batteries are the most common form found in EVs today, ranging from the Nissan Leaf to Mercedes-Benz EQS . No, its not. China is the largest EV market in the world as of now accounting to about 57% of the global EV sales. The source mentioned below: https://www.zdnet.com/article/chinas...ing-ev-market/ https://inverted.in/blog/lithium-pri...the-nmc-market China is betting heavily on the LFP chemistry and thus majority of the cars there are using the same. Even American companies are switching to LFP chemistry owing to their superior life and the ability to withstand harsher conditions. For example, Tesla switched to LFP chemistry for Model 3 and Y. They are also thinking of getting the blade batteries from BYD in the future. https://electrek.co/2022/08/11/tesla...actory-berlin/ https://www.engineering.com/story/wh...-lfp-batteries Please refer the above article. It clearly the explains the advantages and disadvantages of both NMC and LFP batteries. Trying to summarise them below: Advantages of NMC: 1) Higher energy density (around 260Wh/kg), which will lead to reduction in weight when compared the LFP counterparts. 2) Has better charging efficiency under freezing temperatures Disadvantages of NMC 1) Shorter battery life (around 1000 cycles before reaching 80% to 90% of the charge holding capacity) 2) Expensive 3) Presence of metals such as Nickel, Manganese and Cobalt, which lead to sustainability issues. 4) Poor resistance to extreme temperatures. 5) Has lower ignition point and thus has higher chances of thermal runaway under extreme temperatures Advantages of LFP: 1) Cheaper compared to NMC 2) Absence of costlier metals 3) Better resistance to higher temperature 4) Higher lifespan (around 3000 cycles before reducing to 90% of capacity) 5) Better suited to handle high-performance driving and quick charging. Disadvantages of LFP: 1) Poor charging efficiency under sub-zero temperatures. 2) Lower energy density (160 Wh/km to 200 Wh/km) If we take the examples you have mentioned above, there are two factors which justify the NMC adoption: 1) Mercedes and Tesla (Model S and X) are high end performance oriented EVs. For them the battery weight plays a major role. Thus in order to have significant reduction in weight they have gone with NMC. Also the substantial increase in the battery price is not much of a concern since they are already in the higher price bracket. 2) They are mostly being sold in western countries where the climate is colder and rarely reaches the high temperature band where NMCs tend to undergo thermal runaway or deteriorate faster. But they will still end up having lesser life compared to their LFP counterparts. Clearly, LFP is the best fit for low end EV products, that too for a tropical country like India, where temperatures in the summer reach north of 40 degrees Celsius. Quote: Adjustable regen modes might not be there but without the test drive how will you know the tuning of the regen? Who knows Mahindra may offer it via a software update later just as TATA has done with the TIGOR which is getting new features via software update. Trust me, adjustable regen is very useful, irrespective of the regen tuning. In my Ather, the regen is set to certain limit and is not very strong. We can just slow down the scooter using regen but cannot bring it to a complete halt through the same as the it cuts off below 20 km/h. Thus I use regen to slow down the scooter till 20 km/h and then use the friction brakes to bring it to a complete halt. Recently, I test drove the Tigor EV and the Nexon EV. The Tigor EV does not have adjustable regen setting and the regen force at best was adequate to slow down the vehicle. But Nexon completely surprised me. I was testing the sports mode and there was a bad patch of road in some distance ahead of me. Instead of using the brakes to slow down, I tried the level 3 regen, and to my surprise, the deceleration was such that it could easily bring the vehicle to a stand still without the help of friction brakes. In the entire test drive of the Nexon EV, I rarely used the traditional brakes. Also the feature to turn off regen is very helpful on highway trips. For example, I can turn off regen in the highway during cruising. When I want to slow down, say there is a toll booth ahead, I can just set it to level 2 or 3 and recover much of the energy while slowing down, rather than using the friction brakes. Thus its a very desirable feature every EV must have. The adjustable regen setting gives the freedom to adjust the braking force as per your driving style. As you said, they can give it as an update, provided there is a hardware support. This is difficult with Tigor EV as there are no toggle buttons which can be used to achieve it, contrary to Nexon. Eager to see what approach Tata takes to implement it on the Tigor. But when you know that many are appreciating the regen level adjustment feature on the Nexon, why not take inputs from that and provide it upfront during launch, rather than providing it as an update? Mahindra had earlier provided a button to adjust the EPS feedback on the ICE XUV300. Why not provide a similar feature for regen? I wish Mahindra implements this somehow before the launch in January. Quote: I would wait for the vehicle to come to showrooms and take a test drive. Lets give an Indian manufacturer manufacturing and engineering and designing in India a chance just as we are rejoicing an Indian origin person becoming PM of a country. Definitely we can wait for the showroom drives for the technical points discussed above. What I am disappointed is with the hurried unveil of the vehicle while its still under development and also no mention of the price. Even Toyota did the same with the Hyryder when it came to not announcing the price during the launch. I am all in for supporting Indian brands. In fact, I had very high hopes on the XUV400 and was very eagerly waiting for its launch. Probably that's what caused the disappointment. I am extremely happy that Indian companies are leading the EV market in the country in both two wheeler and the four wheeler segment. That's the same reason I have decided to go with only the Indian brands for my purchase of the EV (just my personal opinion). I was one of the early adopters of the Ather and I had booked mine in 2019, that too when there was no Ather service centre in my city (still its not there) and the nearest service centre was 280 km away in Bengaluru. Many questioned my decision of going with the Ather when it was still an unproven product, but I went ahead with my decision and I am enjoying every bit of it . This was mainly because of the confidence they gave me with their product during the launch. Fun fact, Ather has NMC battery. But at that time, I knew nothing about batter chemistry and there was no other option in the market for a well built electric scooter. Till now, in my 3 years and 4 months of ownership, I have clocked about 11k plus km and have utilized 194 cycles out of the 1000. Since I have lower usage and a lot of battery cycles left, the battery chemistry is not bothering me much. But same cannot be said about cars which have higher usage and frequent fast charging cycles.  Even today, if I am in the market for a bike , I will blindly put my money on the Ultraviolette F77 for the same reason of the confidence they provide. They are launching their product only after their manufacturing line is completely ready. On the contrary, Simple One did not help much with this aspect for me. I wholeheartedly wish that Mahindra succeeds with both the XUV400 and their future BE vehicles, because at the end of the day, what I would desire is a healthy competition between the Indian brands, which will ensure that they remain competitive in the budding EV market and fight the Chinese onslaught.

Dont want this to become a counter argument debate, so summing up, Why should we not trust our own engineers at MnM if they have put their faith in NMC? Why adopt herd mentality always? Why follow China and USA? Who knows MnM may have achieved a fine balance of cost range endurance reliability with the NMC batteries. Lets wait. I think as consumers we can only comment on products for sale or concepts previewed. Why MnM is taking a particular decision, why only previewing products etc etc is all business and market dependent. As consumers we can demand what we want but not really have any say on the process how. And in the end ultimately consumer is king, we like it we take it else reject it. I feel its time we wait for Indian engineers to create miracles for Indian companies and we must back it. All that NMC vs LFP is fine but innovation is continual process and who know what MnM has in store. Forget media drives with some features disabled, we have had many threads on Tata EV issues post sales. ICE world, we have so many recalls or failures. That isn't half baked. I am not patronizing MnM only but the Auto Manufacturing as a whole. Lets be positive is my conclusion. Lets wait and watch and hope for the best.

text: A recent trademark filing by Mahindra suggests that the electric version of the XUV700 could be called the XEV 7e. The XEV 7e will be a non-coupe version of the XEV 9e, and as the latest leaked images suggest, its overall design will be similar to the XUV700. While the bodyshell looks identical, the front fascia will be inspired by Mahindra’s pure-electric line-up. The XEV 7e has vertically stacked LED headlamps with L-shaped LED DRLs connected by an LED light bar. The grille will be replaced with a blank fascia featuring an illuminated logo in the centre. The rear end has a more familiar design with L-shaped taillights and black trim in the centre. The XEV 7e also gets a panoramic sunroof, a powered tailgate, black alloy wheels and ORVMs. Inside, the XEV 7e will get the same dashboard as the XEV 9e. It has a triple-screen layout and a 2-spoke steering wheel. Moreover, the XEV 7e will have 3-row seating with captain seats in the second row. As per the leaked images, the XEV 7e could be offered in a dual-motor configuration with multiple traction modes. Source: Rushlane Link to Team-BHP news

Quote: Originally Posted by DeKay Why are you worried? Either way, whether it's 500+ or 480, you won't be getting that range in general driving anyway. My 60kWH Atto 3 shows 460 on a full charge, but it's really tough to get even 400 in the real world. Just don't think about it, enjoy your car! I think you misread my question. It was not about how many kms I can get out of the car. It was simply to ask if the battery capacity is as is, and the reduced range on the dash is because of the update or that the capacity has reduced and shows a top line only for 480 kms. All of it independent of the fact how many kms I get out of it. But I agree. In summers (with the AC on), I get about 380-400 kms with 10-15% battery still left, and recently since winters have started and AC is mostly off, I am able to fetch 420-440kms with 10-15% still left. My last trip for reference, with complete circuit throughout the week being Gurgaon-Noida-Gurgaon, was 430 kms and I had 16% battery still left at the end of the week and the car was driven on Eco mode only. So I think it is fine as long as you have a soft foot.

Hi guys, Have anybody had issues with the 7.2 kW Relux charger that came with Atto3 after the facelift? I had sold my charger to one of my colleagues and within 15 months, the charger has issues with the circuit board and is burning up MCBs & fuses. The Relux guys say the warranty period is over and the board cannot be replaced as it is an older model and no longer available. The only option is replacing the charger. Wanted to know if this was a one off incident.

Hi guys. Our BYD Atto 3's second service got over last week. Atto 3 service interval is once a year or every 20k kms, whichever comes first. Our car had covered 16,000 km since the last sevice. The service cost came to Rs.3040! The car had a fender displacement due to a minor accident and that was also corrected. Total expense was around Rs.12k. Only downside was that the body work took 3 days.The body shop is at a different location from the service centre which added to the time. Availability of spare parts with BYD is an issue. The spare parts took almost 1 month to arrive. On a lighter note,I had asked the front wipers to be replaced. But on checking the invoice, I saw that they had replaced the rear wiper! On checking with them, the service technician told me that the front wipers had some more life left but the rear wiper was almost done. He told me to change the front wipers just before monsoon as the summers play havoc with the wipers. This is the first time during the ownership of all my cars that a rear wiper was replaced. It is a notoriously difficult item to get hold off! Even Maruti did not have it in stock when I asked specifically for it for our Ignis. Did one or two cosmetic enhancements for the car. A carbon fibre fender badge. And a lower front grill That's all for now. Cheers!

source_target: 2022 MG ZS EV Facelift Review - Page 3
text: The ZS-EV does look like a compelling proposition for those with mostly in-city driving and highway runs of  say 150 KM on each side, or upto 300KM each side if one is guaranteed overnight charging facility. My highway runs to my holiday cottage in the hills are like 520KM each way and EV is not an answer, unless I plan overnight halts at hotels with charging capabilities. I also hate to take forced breaks at charging stations over breakfast etc. as I have variable start times on most trips and it is hard to plan the trips too cleanly. Many times, you are returning on a Sunday afternoon, doing an 8 hour drive and must be at work Monday morning, and cannot afford unplanned stops due to charging stations being unavailable etc. Even if one planned an overnight halt for charging, then again, with unreliable power, I always worry of they had a blackout what would I do the next morning I wish the EVs like MG ZS would be designed with battery swapping facility like we are speaking of now for 2 and 3 wheelers in the city. This would provide a lot more peace of mind, as the experience is essentially like pulling into a fuel station and topping off your tank. I guess this would require a large amount of standardization across manufacturers who are already off to different specs, design and sizes for the battery.

source_target: 2022 MG ZS EV Facelift Review - Page 3
text: Quote: Originally Posted by 84.monsoon The ZS-EV does look like a compelling proposition for those with mostly in-city driving and highway runs of  say 150 KM on each side, or upto 300KM each side if one is guaranteed overnight charging facility. My highway runs to my holiday cottage in the hills are like 520KM each way and EV is not an answer, unless I plan overnight halts at hotels with charging capabilities. I also hate to take forced breaks at charging stations over breakfast etc. as I have variable start times on most trips and it is hard to plan the trips too cleanly. Many times, you are returning on a Sunday afternoon, doing an 8 hour drive and must be at work Monday morning, and cannot afford unplanned stops due to charging stations being unavailable etc. Even if one planned an overnight halt for charging, then again, with unreliable power, I always worry of they had a blackout what would I do the next morning I wish the EVs like MG ZS would be designed with battery swapping facility like we are speaking of now for 2 and 3 wheelers in the city. This would provide a lot more peace of mind, as the experience is essentially like pulling into a fuel station and topping off your tank. I guess this would require a large amount of standardization across manufacturers who are already off to different specs, design and sizes for the battery. An EV might not be ideal for you if you have such requirements and preferences. Personally, for me, I would need a few breaks on a 500 plus km journey and charging won’t be an issue. Battery swapping is not a workable idea at all. Would you be ok with swapping your 12 plus lakh battery that is at a 100% SOH (state of health) with an unknown one which might have 20% degradation? What if your battery is only a few months old but the swapped one is 5 years old? This only works with fleets like taxis which run in a small pre determined area. In addition, setting up a charger is easy and cheap. Any hotel, restaurant, rest area or business can easily do so. So you would have thousands of options. If you were limited to swapping, there would be very few stations in some limited areas. Then there are all kinds of problems that would crop up. Imagine a power failure in the middle of the swapping process or some other complication. Would there be trained mechanics available 24/7 to handle this? The area required, the equipment and the cost is far more than a simple charger. Now with chargers available at rates as high as 350KW, you can charge a vehicle in 10 minutes. So swapping is a complete non starter. Some companies might try it by selling a cheaper vehicle with battery as a service concept where you won’t own or pay for the battery. It might work in commercial vehicles and some basic budget EVs. It won’t work for normal personal use. A taxi driver doesn’t want to spend an hour out of this 10 hour shift on charging each day. A private owner doing an occasional trip has no such constraints.

source_target: 2022 MG ZS EV Facelift Review - Page 3
text: I don't think they have LFP batteries in the Indian version. as per my discussion with mg technical person its mmc battery here. The previous version for sure had mmc. Even I was thinking that as per the UK version this should have LFP here due to extreme weather and lower cost and the fact that 100% charging needed. And also that its life is much longer than NMC. When people drive electric cars for 4-5 years or 150000 km and find out the battery degradation in NMC batteries and have to replace batteries that till put off the whole EV market. By that time the market would have been taken over by blade batteries and these cars / batteries would be redundant. The technical person clearly said there is still no option to repair any battery cell in the mg battery in India due to cost and designing in the Indian version. if any one cell goes the whole battery will be changed. The battery kind is written under the seat. If you have the car kindly check it and send a picture so everyone can know the exact specifications of the new battery. This was done by another forum member in UK. You might need to do some carpet removing to find it. That will tell you if you need to charge it to 20-80% to preserve health in case it mmc or 100% if it's LFP. I think the technical people in MG are not really bothered to tell everyone that its NMC and the 20-80 % range is only about 150-170 if used properly to keep the health. The battery management system in NMC has been modified in UK to select the maximum charge to 80 or 90 or 50 % as per your need. Not so in LFP. please check and post a picture of the cell type. Thanks.

The front row section and dashboard of the XEV 9S resemble those of the XEV 9E. However, the following differences make it not identical :- Leather colour scheme on the steering wheel, 9S got it complete black, and 9S has a black+green mix. Very obvious, 9S gets electric controls for the Co-Driver seat on the doorpad and the seat. A proper sun-visor in 9S as compared to 1-by-2 sun-visor on 9E. The mirror and light on the sun-visor are also different. The sunroof control console is totally different, 9S benefits from a sunglass holder.

The package is attractive but the integration is where more work needs to be done 1: Panel gaps on display vehicle and door welds were rough (I'm nit picking) 2: Having driven the Harrier EV briefly, it was a smoother and felt more resolved. The 9S fell short on this front. 3: The ergonomics were not perfect with the steering controls. Until one is familiar, one needs to look down since one cannot feel the respective control 4: Weird electric window switches - lift up for window to go down and vice versa.
Many of the fellow bhpians would have this in mind. How does it compete with the clavis ev HTX+ LR. It has a smaller battery and does about 350 kms, but comparing top to top its 5 lacs cheaper.

Even then, the sales figures of the 7OO have always been unbelievable! One of the reasons behind 7OO thumping success was the features the car offered. Today if any car over 15L doesn't have one of these - ADAS, Sunroof or Powerful motor option, it's almost Dead on arrival. With the XEV 9S, Mahindra is targeting a niche EV market segment, where it doesn't have to fight with car makers from smaller segments (Nexon, Windsor, e3, etc). Mahindra is offering a proper comfortable SUV with electric motor. Mahindra is known for 20L+ SUVs, and have already shown that they can make competent EVs too, and with Mahindra's experience of servicing cars in 20L segment, this should be another winner for them. Quote: Originally Posted by Omkar The transmission mode selector has a simple design, but I didn’t find it very easy to use. It doesn’t have dedicated slots for different modes; instead, it returns to the center position after you move it up or down. There’s a button on the steering wheel to engage "One-Pedal Drive". Once engaged, the regeneration level is automatically cranked up to Level 3. I feel Team-BHP needs to revamp their EV reviews since most of it is just copy paste from earlier EV reviews. It’s surprising that the same monostable gear selector is appreciated in Harrier EV and mentioned as an annoyance in XEV 9S. Audi A4 and BMW 330Li also have monostable gear selectors and I think most modern cars with the exception of Toyotas (which anyway use ancient technology) use monostable gear selectors. Also, there was no mention of auto regen in the review and one pedal drive is showcased as level 3 regen. In reality, these cars have 6 regen modes - Level 0, Level 1, Level 2, Level 3, one pedal drive & auto regen. Even the augmented reality part in heads up display was completely skipped. The HUD shows AR direction arrows on google maps through both Apple Carplay and Android Auto. Then, there of no mention of how the semi active suspension with frequency selective damping affect ride and handling in different drive modes. The suspension gets softer or stiffer as per the drive mode selected. Lastly, there was no mention of the amazing ADAS system on these cars. Did Team-BHP not get enough time with this car?

I feel that Team-BHP's EV reviews are way better than what some other big players in this car critic space provide. This may be due to the fact that our moderators do not accept any money from the carmakers. Quote: Originally Posted by beemer5499 It’s surprising that the same monostable gear selector is appreciated in Harrier EV and mentioned as an annoyance in XEV 9S. I think most modern cars use monostable gear selectors. The gear selector in the Harrier EV is similar to a conventional one, with the lever moving generously, and the only gripe being its lightweight feel. The one in the XEV 9S has limited room for movement, thereby making it cumbersome to use. And, many modern cars do come with a proper physical gear lever, and not using the shift-by-wire technology. Quote: Then, there's of no mention of how the semi active suspension with frequency selective damping affect ride and handling in different drive modes. The suspension gets softer or stiffer as per the drive mode selected. Lastly, there was no mention of the amazing ADAS system on these cars. Did Team-BHP not get enough time with this car? The semi active suspension is not essentially similar to adaptive dampers found on cars costing way more. They are just stated as 'changing their stiffness based on road conditions'. Nowhere was it mentioned that it alters the damping based on drive mode. Please do not spread these wrong information. The reviewers have spent quality time with the car, and their records are first-hand observations, not mere speculations from the spec sheet or youtube videos.

Quote: Originally Posted by sunsivj More particular my question or thoughts are around the charging station and types. Do they vary based on each car? If they are designed to support any car model, I think that's more practical. Also once I invest in a good fast charger and have set it up, it should be supportive to use any car model and customers should not be forced to buy a new one each time. I may even share my charging station between two of my electric cars or may share it with a neighbour/friend. Charger cost - is this free from insurance cost, road tax, additional Cess? If yes and buying a charger is compulsory, then each time we end up paying more money with no proper justification to some of the taxes. All EVs sold in India use the same CCS2 charger so the charger cables are compatible across them. A Hyundai Kona charger cable can be used to charge any Tata EV for example. All fast charging stations or slow charging stations hence supports all the EVs on the road. So anything you setup privately too will. Currently charging cable comes free with the car or rather you don't pay anything separately for them. Eventually car manufacturers may stop giving charging cables just as the phone manufacturers have. But that scenario will be atleast a decade away. Third party cables cost around 15K currently and will decrease over time. Tata's cable costs around 30K which seems to be extra marked up. The charging cables are covered under warranty and you will only need to shell out any money to buy only after warranty is over. Cheers

Quote: Originally Posted by Cresterk I don't get it. Why can't we just hold the brakes and then press the accelerator like we have been doing for the past 50 years on hills? There is not even a clutch to juggle and the power delivery is immediate so no waiting for the clutch to engage etc either like in an AMT. It is a dimwit drive by wire system. If the brakes are pressed, even if you floor the accelerator, no power will go to the motor because of the brake input sensor. When you release the brakes, there is a momentary delay after which the car registers your accelerator input and decides to give the requested power to the motor.

Today morning I got a call from the nearest Mahindra Showroom informing me that they have unofficially started the booking for 9S. I can initiate the booking by giving a PDC (Post Dated Cheque) for 14-Jan-2026 for an amount of INR 21,000/- (fully refundable). On asking how & when the booking will be done in the system, she said that on 14-Jan-2025, the booking window will be opened, and they will enter the bookings of all those customers who have pre-booked with PDCs. M&M is expecting the first lot booking to get full soon and the waiting period may increase. The deliveries will start from 26-Jan-2026. I told her that I am in no hurry and want the car by mid-2026 (June end to be precise). Also told her that I can do the booking only after I am convinced with the car (after my test drive). She said that yesterday she had been to Mumbai to attend the dealer training for 9S. On asking if that was only theoretical or practical as well, she said that she did get to drive the car and it was amazing. It drives exactly like the XEV 9E (but a tad slower due to the heavier kerb weight). I asked her to get the exact weight details of both the cars. She also said that she can arrange a test drive of the 9E at my home tomorrow, i.e. Saturday, so that I can check if it fits in my garage parking. I agreed to it as the 9E is longer that 9S & it can give me a good indication. During conversation the customer exec told me that the second-row seat ventilation is available from Pack-2 itself. I told her that it was available only in Pack-3 & Pack-3 above, but she insisted that it is available from Pack-2 onwards. I didn't contest it given the fact that she had attended the 9S training just yesterday. Now when I checked in the brochure, it is clearly saying "Ventilated 2nd Row Seats in Pack-3 & Pack-3 above". The Mahindra Customer execs need to do their proper homework otherwise they are likely to provide false info to the customers (although unknowingly)

Quote: Originally Posted by antz.bin I wonder how the XUV managed to get the longer range considering it packs in ~1 KWH less battery capacity compared to the Nexon. XUV 400 uses NMC battery, which has higher energy density, so will be lighter compared to Nexon's LFP battery and this translates to higher range even with slightly smaller capacity. On the flip side LFP is more thermally stable and offers many more charge discharge cycles before capacity degrades. So Nexon with LFP offers better safety and longer battery life. Most other car makers are moving to LFP, original MG ZS was NMC but switched to LFP with 2022 MG ZS, the current Kona sold in India is some kind of polymer NMC, but newer Hyundai and Kia models are LFP. But Mahindra decided to go with NMC battery for XUV 400.

Quote: Originally Posted by wocanak XUV 400 uses NMC battery, which has higher energy density, so will be lighter compared to Nexon's LFP battery and this translates to higher range even with slightly smaller capacity. The efficiency is not just due to heavier LFP batteries. Motor efficiency also plays an important role. Hyundai Kona always had a much better rated and real-world range than the original MG ZS EV (NMC). The motor in XUV400 is from Valeo (erstwhile Valeo Siemens) and it's a very refined, efficient motor with good performance. Quote: Originally Posted by wocanak On the flip side LFP is more thermally stable and offers many more charge discharge cycles before capacity degrades. So Nexon with LFP offers better safety and longer battery life. Most other car makers are moving to LFP, original MG ZS was NMC but switched to LFP with 2022 MG ZS, the current Kona sold in India is some kind of polymer NMC, but newer Hyundai and Kia models are LFP. But Mahindra decided to go with NMC battery for XUV 400. The major reason for car companies going for LFP batteries is the cost and availability. Tesla started this trend of using LFP batteries in low-end variants of Model 3 and Model Y as those batteries are cheaper than NMC and available in huge capacities due to Chinese companies' (CATL, BYD, CALB, etc...) large-scale production. Hyundai/Kia uses NMC batteries (sourced from LG Chem and SK Innovation) in all of their models across the world including India. Only in China, they are using LFP batteries.

I don't know why Tata and Mahindra don't offer 50 or 60 kWh battery like MG and BYD. The cost increase really would not be that huge, maybe 2 L more, but the range anxiety of real world 250-300 KM with smaller batteries (30-40 kWh) is gone completely, and customers can use dirt cheap " home " charging instead of locating "outside" expensive charging plugs which may or may not be functional. Larger battery just gives more peace of mind ! This will be useful for taxis and fleets as well, as I see more and more taxi operators choose EV 's due to really low running cost.

Quote: Originally Posted by vtires2018 I don't know why Tata and Mahindra don't offer 50 or 60 kWh battery like MG and BYD. The cost increase really would not be that huge, maybe 2 L more, but the range anxiety of real world 250-300 KM with smaller batteries (30-40 kWh) is gone completely, and customers can use dirt cheap " home " charging instead of locating "outside" expensive charging plugs which may or may not be functional. Larger battery just gives more peace of mind ! This will be useful for taxis and fleets as well, as I see more and more taxi operators choose EV 's due to really low running cost. If you look at the Nexon Max, Tata has squeezed as much as possible. Anymore kwh will require a car with larger wheel base. I guess the Harrier and Sierra will come with packs upto 100kwh. In ICE vehicles with high ground clearance, a EV converted variant can double stack the battery to get double capacity at the cost of ground clearance. Placing the motor and inverter away from the wheelbase side and in horizontal stack, will also give us a frunk. Sadly the off the shelf motor/VCU are all vertical, so a lot of space is wasted. Only US car companies give huge frunk sizes. In DIY EV conversions using Tesla LDU/SDU, they are able to fill up the engine bay area full of batteries. These conversions while road legal in many western countries don't need to be crash tested, where as OEM conversion has to be crash tested. So that is the reason we don't see batteries under the bonnet/Hood from car makers, instead its between the wheelbase.

Quote: Originally Posted by vtires2018 I don't know why Tata and Mahindra don't offer 50 or 60 kWh battery like MG and BYD. The cost increase really would not be that huge, maybe 2 L more, but the range anxiety of real world 250-300 KM with smaller batteries (30-40 kWh) is gone completely, and customers can use dirt cheap " home " charging instead of locating "outside" expensive charging plugs which may or may not be functional. Larger battery just gives more peace of mind ! This will be useful for taxis and fleets as well, as I see more and more taxi operators choose EV 's due to really low running cost. The MAIN reason is the Law of Diminishing returns, i.e, increasing the battery capacity does not increase the range in a linear manner. For every additional kWh, the kms gained keeps decreasing. Batteries are heavy. Very heavy. As a rule of thumb, every 10 kWh battery capacity will add an additional weight of 65 kg to the car. So, if a 1465 kg Nexon EV Max with a 40 kWh battery has a real world range of 360 kms, a Nexon EV with a 60 kWh battery will weigh 1595 kg and will have a range of only 496 km! Just a 37.5% increase in the range for a 50% increase in battery capacity! Worse, hypothetically, if we load a Nexon EV with a 80 kWh battery pack, it's weight increases to 1725 kg and the range increases to just 611 kms. I e, a 25% increase in range for a 33.33% increase in battery capacity. Frankly not worth carrying around 260 kg of extra load on a daily basis(equal to the weight of 4 adults) for an additional range of 250 kms, which we may use only once in 3-4 months. Moreover, the 30 kWh battery of Nexon EV prime takes 9 hours 10 mins to charge to 100% using a 3.3 kW charger (as per the brochure). In Nexon EV Max, the 40 kWh battery takes 15-16 hours to charge to 100% with the same charger. Hence it will be reasonable to assume that a 60 kWh battery will need at least 28-30 hours to charge (from 0-100%) using a 3.3 kW charger. No way this is going to help alleviate any range anxiety. A 40 kWh battery may improve the ride quality, but a 60 or 80 kWh battery is going to put severe strain on the chassis, suspension, wheels and will lead to tyre bursts if we happen to drive over a huge pothole at 100 kmph. Not worth the risk at all! The additional weight will affect the performance and will be a headache while climbing uphill and while overtaking on highways. So the car will need a bigger motor, which will add to the cost and will reduce the range. Even cars like Harrier EV (kerb weight 1400 kg without engine, gearbox and battery pack), hypothetically speaking, with a 100 kWh battery, will weigh about 2050 kgs and will have a real world range of only about 650 kms, against the same vehicle having a range of 500 kms with a 70 kWh battery, and about 195 kgs lighter. And... The cost! Batteries are crazy expensive. 2 years back a Tata sales executive told me that the cost of the 30 kWh  battery pack was about Rs 7 lakhs. Not sure about the cost now. But it is safe to assume that a 60 kWh battery pack alone can cost between Rs 10-15 lakhs today! I would rather live with range anxiety ��

Here we can clearly see that, across manufacturers and models,  the battery weight remains fairly constant at about 70-75kg per 10 kWh. Based on the above mentioned points, I feel, rather than longing for cars with a huge battery, we should be satisfied with a 300-400 km real world range plus inductive charging on the highways. Most highways in India have abundant sunshine throughout the year and inductive charging is very much feasible/practical here.

Quote: Originally Posted by Geta Based on the above mentioned points, I feel, rather than longing for cars with a huge battery, we should be satisfied with a 300-400 km real world range plus inductive charging on the highways. Keep in mind that only 70% of the real world is usable because you want to keep at least 10% as reserve and fast charging is fast only up to 80%. So this makes the max 100% real world range required to be ~570. That will also provide room for spirited ICE like driving. I think very few cars will target range beyond that and this is what most ground up EV platform cars are targeting. Since the cars in India are smaller, they maybe able to provide this range within ~2000 kg.

Finally received my Nexon EV LR Empowered+ Booking process: Booked via nearest tata showroom on the day when bookings opened. Gave Rs. 11000. I was pleasantly surprised to know from SA that I will receive it in around 1 week as it is in transit. There was a family function on 25 Sept, so pushed Sales Advisor to deliver on 24th. Car reached showroom on 24 and we went there to do all the paperwork done. We had booked a fancy number. We were told that it would be difficult to get the car on 24 due to fancy number. Fee had to be paid on the portal before we can get the car. It then got deferred to the next day. We told the SA that if we don't get the car by 1 pm, we won't take the delivery. He said it will be fine. We reached showroom aapne 11. The guy who had to take the file to RTO office, went there at 12.30. However we were conveniently told that he went before we reached Showroom. Then it was lunch time at the RTO office. Finally when we escalated the issue, we came to know that it was possible to get the car without getting the RTO fees paid since we have the number allotment letter. We finally received it at 4:30. Nd we were late to a function at our home Now about the car Design: - Tata motors have exceeded the expectations this time. What a car they have made this time. Everyone at my home and office just loves how it looks. - fit and finish issue is like the lady love of Tata motors and it is there to stay Interiors: - The creamish colour in the interior makes the car look expensive and airy. However, it would require a lot of regular cleaning. No more momos chutney on the seat now - The screen is just so lovely. Quite responsive and picture quality is great. And on top of that the audio quality is sublime. I am planning to watch a movie in the car itself due to the theatre like audio experience that I will get there - Ventilated seats are such a refreshing addition. Driving experience: This is the first time ever I drove an EV and I didn't feel like driving an alien tech. - The smooth and linear pickup is just awesome. - I feel city mode is just perfect. I can sedately drive around the city and the power is there for those quick overtakes. - Sport mode is there to satisfy your adrenaline rush. Once in a while just do pedal to the metal and you will have fun. Also, a good way to teach kids Newton's law of motion - I started off with regen level 1. However by the time I reached Gurugram from Delhi, regen level 3 had become my best friend in the traffic. - Now I drive in regen level 2 in moderate traffic and regen level 3 in stop start traffic. This is only keeping comfort in mind and not  the range. Range: Now addressing elephant in the room - I am getting a range of 280km while driving in traffic. And by traffic I mean an average speed of 15- 20km/hr, with AC on all the time. Average power consumption being 145wh/km. - Didn't get a chance to drive in better conditions, but will update when I do. However, I saw significant drop in Power consumption while driving at 70km/h for 5 km. It went from 145 to 138 wh/km in just 5 km for a trip of 40km.

I wanted to provide an update regarding a rapid battery drain issue I experienced in my Nexon.EV Empowered Plus (40 kWh Long Range). This rapid decline in State of Charge (SoC) occurred immediately after a specific warning event: The car was in Park mode with the handbrake engaged. I stepped out for a minute to drop my child at the school gate, with the keys in my pocket. The car began to honk multiple times, signaling a warning that occurs when the key is not recognized upon exiting the vehicle. When I returned, the car failed to recognize the key, even though it was still in my pocket. Following this sequence of events, there was a sharp decline in the vehicle's SoC. (On a lighter note, perhaps Tata Motors has a security feature that aggressively drops the SoC if the key isn't recognized, to thwart potential theft!)

Dear Fellow BHPians, I am posting this with a heavy heart because I genuinely love the Tata brand and everything it represents for Indian engineering. However, what my family has experienced with our Tata Nexon EV over the past several weeks has been harrowing — and I feel it is my responsibility to this community to document it in full, both so that other owners are informed and so that there is a clear public record of what has transpired. Vehicle: Tata Nexon EV (Registration: KL74C5988) Location: Kozhikode, Kerala Service Centre: Luxon Tata, Kozhikode --- THE ISSUE --- The vehicle has been afflicted by a recurring "HV System Fault — Contact Service Centre" error that renders the car completely unable to engage Drive or Reverse. The car simply dies in whichever mode it is in, with no warning, and will not move. This is not a one-time occurrence. Here is the full timeline: 1. The fault first struck without warning, stranding my family in live traffic. The car was towed to Luxon Tata, Kozhikode. 2. Tata Motors replaced the high-voltage (HV) battery under warranty at no cost. We assumed the issue was resolved. 3. The IDENTICAL fault has now recurred — at approximately 30% State of Charge — stranding us again. Same error. Same symptoms. Same dangerous situation. A battery replacement clearly did not address the root cause. No written Root Cause Analysis (RCA) was ever provided after the first repair. We were simply handed back the car and told it was fixed. --- TATA MOTORS' RESPONSE --- The service centre's proposed resolution this time: a) Replace the HV battery again at no cost (same fix that already failed once) b) Charge us separately for a compressor issue that they claim is unrelated Both points are unacceptable. On point (a): Replacing the same part for the same fault without identifying the root cause is not a repair. It is an experiment — conducted on our safety and at our inconvenience. On point (b): The compressor of this vehicle was functioning perfectly throughout our ownership, with zero complaints, until the moment this HV System Fault struck. There was no independent mechanical failure of the compressor. It has not been subjected to any accident, misuse, or unusual stress. The compressor issue surfaced alongside the HV fault and is almost certainly a consequence of it. To ask us to pay for this is, frankly, an attempt to transfer the liability of a systemic product failure onto the consumer. We have categorically refused to pay for the compressor. --- ESCALATION STEPS TAKEN --- We have exhausted every reasonable avenue of dialogue with Tata Motors at the dealer and customer care level. We have since escalated formally as follows: 1. Written emails to the MD & CEO (Mr. Shailesh Chandra) and the Chairman's Office — three formal notices sent, demanding either a full buyback or complete vehicle replacement. Responses have been routed through customer care without any substantive resolution. 2. MoRTH Vehicle Defect Reporting Portal — a formal complaint has been lodged documenting this as a recurring, life-threatening HV safety defect for consideration under India's mandatory vehicle recall framework. 3. CCPA (Central Consumer Protection Authority) — complaint filed under the Consumer Protection Act, 2019, citing product liability and deficiency in service. 4. e-Daakhil — case filed with the District Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission seeking a full buyback and compensation. 5. We have refused delivery of the vehicle. It remains at the service centre at Tata Motors' risk and liability until a proper resolution is offered. --- WHAT WE ARE ASKING FOR --- We are not asking for another battery. No repair at this point — however thorough — can restore our confidence in the safety of this vehicle. A car that has suffered two major HV system failures, for which no root cause has ever been identified or fixed, is not a car we can put our family in. Our demand is simple and fair: a full vehicle buyback at a just market value, or a complete replacement vehicle with a certified written guarantee that the systemic defect has been permanently resolved. --- QUESTIONS FOR THE COMMUNITY --- 1. Have any other BHPians experienced a recurring HV System Fault on the Nexon EV — particularly one that recurred AFTER a battery replacement under warranty? 2. Has anyone successfully obtained a buyback from Tata Motors for a recurring safety defect? 3. Any members with experience in consumer court / e-Daakhil proceedings against an automaker who can share their experience? I will keep this thread updated with every development including legal proceedings. Any advice from the community is deeply appreciated. Thank you for being the most informed and honest automotive community in India. If this is indeed a batch-level defect in the HV architecture — and based on what I have read on this very forum, I suspect it may be — every Nexon EV owner here deserves to know.

Quote: Originally Posted by NexonEV_OwnerKL Dear Fellow BHPians, --- QUESTIONS FOR THE COMMUNITY --- 1. Have any other BHPians experienced a recurring HV System Fault on the Nexon EV — particularly one that recurred AFTER a battery replacement under warranty? HV System Fault is a catch all error that does not really give any information as to what the actual problem is. Here are some of the things you might want to check 1.  I hope you follow the charging routine as outlined https://www.team-bhp.com/forum/elect...g-routine.html (LFP battery charging routine) .  If you are doing the 20-80% charging (that is largely meant for NMC batteries) with LFP batteries then you are going to get into trouble. A few low SoC to 100% SoC charging should take care of this. 2. Also I would highly recommend that you do the following update. https://www.team-bhp.com/forum/elect...ml#post5821367 (HV Critical Alert in Tata EVs) You will still have to contend with the sudden drop in SoC in  the low SoC range. Many people have not taken the above mentioned VCU update fearing the SoC drop but I think that is much better than the HV critical error which is honestly too dangerous. Quote: Thank you for being the most informed and honest automotive community in India. If this is indeed a batch-level defect in the HV architecture — and based on what I have read on this very forum, I suspect it may be — every Nexon EV owner here deserves to know. I am convinced that every LFP battery pack with cylindrical cells is going to have this sudden SoC drop once you get the VCU update (I myself am suffering this). Instead of getting roughly 3.2 km every soc we start getting 2 km every soc in the low Soc range (10-25%).  If you know about it you can be prepared for it. But it is still many times better than the dangerous  HV critical error. The reason for the SoC drop in cylindrical battery packs has been explained in great detail in the following post. https://www.team-bhp.com/forum/elect...iguration.html (Why you should care about your LFP battery pack configuration?)

text: Quote: Originally Posted by notme Improved cooling requires a larger compressor, which in turn consumes more power and more power usage reduces range. It's a trade off. Technically, you are right.  But, in the afternoon sun, the Windsor is below acceptable comfort right now. A 20% larger compressor's additional energy consumption will be minor compared to the main drive motor's consumption and the 38-52KWh battery sizes. Could even be recovered if MG did a slight recalibration of the Eco mode. Most buyers will be happier with better cooling in this car. Plus, there are two ways to make the Windsor cool and less energy hungry at the same time- 1. Reduce the massive heat load in the car with thermal/IR-cut tinted windows . 70VLT front and back windscreens. (The green tint glass that used to come in the VW Polo would look nice on this car, though we'll be happy with a plain grey as long as it's less hot inside.) 2. Cooled seats front and back. These deliver AC cooling right next to the body, and energy consumption is lower than cooling the entire large cabin down to a low enough temperature. It can leave the cabin cooling as-is/ Mg might get away without increasing the AC compressor capacity. The Windsor can end up with a slightly longer range due to these changes, though you'll see the range impact mainly in the peak summer months or on long afternoon drives. With the Windsor's large and sloping glass house, these are necessary features, not optional extras.  Another car with a smaller glass house and less raked winsdcreen or smaller internal volume may not need it so badly. The strange thing is that, compared to the price of the car, these are not very expensive to implement at factory level, and the price ballooning impact of GST is also missing here.  But, MG has not done these fixes or the rear suspension improvement, even though it will remove 2 major complaints with this car.

Checked out Curvv Ev and ICE versions and here’s the observations from a Taigun user. 1. Car does look good in person, pictures and videos doesn’t do justice to this car. Meanwhile I never liked Nexon styling ev for many reason, especially due to rear quarter and rear end styling. Curvv somehow looks more proportional and coupe rear end matches well with the raising window line. 2. Its not a spacious car! Especially at rear. Rear legroom is limited due to the shorter wheelbase and lacks under thigh support due to the raised floor . Due to slopping roof I was expecting a compromised headroom but the lack of knee room and lack of space to stretch your leg underneath the front seat makes it uncomfortable for long rides for a 6 feet tall person like me. All the extra length of this car went on to the coupe styling and boot which surprisingly is quite good. 3. The dashboard is straight up lifted from nexon, the layout, textures and colors are used tastefully. But there are some scope of improvements in fit and finish, but Tata had improved there game by a huge margin from their previous generation cars. The plastics used are good but not great, could have used some soft touch plastics on the touch points of door and dashboard which would have definitely improved the cabin experience. The red wine color combination needs a shout out, it’s tastefully done. ❤️ 4. Ingress and egress is bit difficult in rear due to the high floor, it becomes especially difficult when the front seat is at its rearmost position. 5. Range on paper looks good, seems adequate for the price but it depends on the use case. 6. Some Quality and Ergonomics issues. The HVAC control is not great, its very inconsistent in operation, steering wheel looks impressive but the controls are not properly placed IMHO. On to the exterior, the black claddings are gonna bring huge sales to PPF folks, and potential customers should take your Curvv ev to a PPF shop soon after you are out of showroom. Overall I liked the car, it’s good. But unfortunately the lack of rear legroom is something I cannot accept in a C segment car, I can live with the slopping roof and compromised headroom but legroom is something very much important for a long distance comfortable travel in my personal opinion. But apart from this shortcomings Curvv is a promising product and may be even can become one of Tata’s hot sellers in no time.

Quote: Originally Posted by elevator Please help me understand, how Nexon or Punch will be able to get more range during fast charging compared to Tata Curvv? This is from my experience of extensively driving the ev across India. From Leh to Kanyakumari and east. Most of the Tata and BPCL chargers are 30 KW fast chargers. 60KW chargers form Jio and others usually have two charging slots, if one vehicle is there then it would charge at 60 KW, if there are two vehicles it would split to 30 KW each. Only Zeon has dedicated 50KW chargers. Chargezone also has few. There are only few dedicated chargers of 70KW or higher. Even if you get 60 KW charging, chances are there that another vehicle would join and start charging in between. Tata Curve real world mileage is about 400 kms. Reviewed got 360-390, so careful driving would give 400+. Assuming 55kwh gives 400 kms, for 100 percent, we would get 4kms per 1 percent. If we are charging at say 30 KW charger, in one hour we would top up 30/55 * 100 or 54 percent. That would provide about 220 kms of range. In same 30 KW charger, you would be able to charge EV max or punch ev lr to almost 100 percent. EV max would provide 300-320 kms of highway range, punch ev may provide 270+ kms of range. Curvv ev is definitely a much better product with higher charging speed and battery capacity. However at the moment highway infrastructure is not there to realise the full benefit. So please consider this while making your purchase. As per your use case. I have driven 800 kms also in one day in MAX. A higher range and higher charging speed definitely helps, only if you have access to higher capacity dedicated chargers. Otherwise, considering you stop for breakfast, lunch evening snacks and dinner, you would manage almost same time in max or curvv. Depends on your route. Entire J&K we have 30 KW chargers. Leh has a DC charger but limited time. Else it is 15A charging everywhere. Entire east difficult to find higher capacity chargers. There are many 25KW chargers also. All 60 KW chargers except a few works with load sharing everywhere else. Zeon has some dedicated 50 KW chargers. 50 KW charging would also take about an hour and half to fully charge.

Quote: Originally Posted by sumannandy This is from my experience of extensively driving the ev across India. From Leh to Kanyakumari and east. Most of the Tata and BPCL chargers are 30 KW fast chargers. 60KW chargers form Jio and others usually have two charging slots, if one vehicle is there then it would charge at 60 KW, if there are two vehicles it would split to 30 KW each. Very well put. I would say people should really evaluate their running before committing to the larger battery pack (and therefore larger car). When planning to get an Nexon EV Max (we did not have much options at that time), we went for it because 300 Kms of effective range should workout for about a week of travel for us (unless we do a long trip). Which effectively means a charging cycle per week. Being in Kerala most of our intercity travel is also of that range. Our social visits for example PKD-Kozhikode-PKD can all be dealt with with a single slow charging  at home. The longest I did was a PKD-TVM-Kanyakumari-TVM-palakkad with a halt at my sisters place in TVM. This meant a single FC between PKD-TVM and and a slow charging at TVM that takes care of the TVM-Kanyakumari-TVM trip (few people were amused to see a KL-09 EV in Kanyakumari actually). If you are planning for an EV do evaluate these very carefully because 1. You are compromising on other things like ergonomics 2. As @sumannandy brilliantly demonstrated, you might end up spending more time charging the bigger EV than the smaller EV. Together with the fact that slow charging to 100% (a must for LFP batteries) is going to take more time you really need to see if the tradeoff is worth taking.

Quote: Originally Posted by anandhsub If the "knees up" position is because of the battery does it mean the ICE Curvv will be better? Or is the floor height common for both? Most likely the ICE will be better. Reason: It’s based on altogether a different platform. The .ev cars (Punch and now Curvv) are based on a new “born EV” platform, while the older Nexon EV and the ICE Curvv are based on the continually updated Indica platform.

I think there is an issue with the calculation. Larger batteries and batteries in general dont charge in a linear fashion as you have calculated. There is a parabolic curve. Slower in first 10..15% .. super fast around 30 to 70 and very slow 90 ..100% So 30/55 x 100 logic wont work. Curvv will still charge faster with more range in the same time with the same charger charger, as punch etc. will hit that 85%..100% band and charge drearily slow, while Curvv would be charging at full throttle being in the band range. Quote: Originally Posted by sumannandy This is from my experience of extensively driving the ev across India. From Leh to Kanyakumari and east. Most of the Tata and BPCL chargers are 30 KW fast chargers. 60KW chargers form Jio and others usually have two charging slots, if one vehicle is there then it would charge at 60 KW, if there are two vehicles it would split to 30 KW each. Only Zeon has dedicated 50KW chargers. Chargezone also has few. There are only few dedicated chargers of 70KW or higher. Even if you get 60 KW charging, chances are there that another vehicle would join and start charging in between. Tata Curve real world mileage is about 400 kms. Reviewed got 360-390, so careful driving would give 400+. Assuming 55kwh gives 400 kms, for 100 percent, we would get 4kms per 1 percent. If we are charging at say 30 KW charger, in one hour we would top up 30/55 * 100 or 54 percent. That would provide about 220 kms of range. In same 30 KW charger, you would be able to charge EV max or punch ev lr to almost 100 percent. EV max would provide 300-320 kms of highway range, punch ev may provide 270+ kms of range. Curvv ev is definitely a much better product with higher charging speed and battery capacity. However at the moment highway infrastructure is not there to realise the full benefit. So please consider this while making your purchase. As per your use case. I have driven 800 kms also in one day in MAX. A higher range and higher charging speed definitely helps, only if you have access to higher capacity dedicated chargers. Otherwise, considering you stop for breakfast, lunch evening snacks and dinner, you would manage almost same time in max or curvv. Depends on your route. Entire J&K we have 30 KW chargers. Leh has a DC charger but limited time. Else it is 15A charging everywhere. Entire east difficult to find higher capacity chargers. There are many 25KW chargers also. All 60 KW chargers except a few works with load sharing everywhere else. Zeon has some dedicated 50 KW chargers. 50 KW charging would also take about an hour and half to fully charge.

Quote: Originally Posted by sumannandy Most of the Tata and BPCL chargers are 30 KW fast chargers. 60KW chargers form Jio and others usually have two charging slots, if one vehicle is there then it would charge at 60 KW, if there are two vehicles it would split to 30 KW each. Only Zeon has dedicated 50KW chargers. Chargezone also has few. There are only few dedicated chargers of 70KW or higher. Even if you get 60 KW charging, chances are there that another vehicle would join and start charging in between. In same 30 KW charger, you would be able to charge EV max or punch ev lr to almost 100 percent. EV max would provide 300-320 kms of highway range, punch ev may provide 270+ kms of range. Curvv ev is definitely a much better product with higher charging speed and battery capacity. However at the moment highway infrastructure is not there to realise the full benefit. So please consider this while making your purchase. As per your use case. Having a larger battery and faster charging speeds is always better than a car with a smaller battery and slower charging speeds, especially when they are priced so close to each other. Any car charging curve starts to taper down after 80 to 85% state of charge (SoC). For example, Nexon EV max maintains 30KW charging only till 83% and comes down to 22% after that. At 92% it goes down to just 11%. With bigger batteries in Currv EV, the capacity and range from 10% to 85% will be larger than the ones in Nexon EV max. Current highway EV chargers may not be good enough but it's improving rapidly. Many of the new charging hubs are all using 50KW+ chargers and many of them have multiple chargers. So, the chances of all of them being occupied at the same time will be less, enabling cars like Currv EV to get full 60KW charging speed. If anything, its the Tata that has kept the industry backwards by sticking with low charging speeds in Nexon and Punch. Also, most of the 30KW chargers are also their own tata power chargers or BPCL. All other major operators like JIO BP, Statiq, Chargezone, Zeon, etc... are installing faster chargers and setting up multiple chargers at the same location. So, the charging infra will be much better in the next 2-3 years and most of them will be faster and higher in number. If anyone is in the market for a TATA EV for > Rs 15 lakhs, then Currv EV is a no-brainer.

There is also the initial drive advantage from home. With Nexon you might need to start looking for charger after 250 km while in Curvv you can go another 70 odd km before looking out for charger. DC fast chargers > 45 kw from plug share. Situation is improving slowly. Still many may find Nexon VFM with the current discounts and if the price stays the same or goes lower.

source_target: 2025 Kia Carens Clavis EV Review - Page 5
text: I am looking for a new car, and my choices are all over the place. My primary use case is going on short trips nearby Pune for mountains and forts. Although my running isn't much - much less than 1000 kms a month - I am still interested in getting an EV, for the following reasons: - None of the automatics can match the smoothness of EVs - Plenty of power, without turbo lag - Cheap running means I'll use my car more However, I am not prepared to make big compromises that many EVs come bundled with. Harrier EV is a prime example. You buy a huge car, and get space equivalent of a Baleno. I had written off Clavis EV on this thread post the price reveal. But decided to check it out yesterday, as it was on the way to a Tata showroom, where I was headed to test drive Nexon EV. Having happily owned a Carens earlier, I am already familiar with the design. So what surprised me was not the differences, but how similar the EV is to the ICE version. Almost the same amount of space everywhere. How did Kia manage this (without a born EV platform), which Tata and Mahindra couldn't? Ans: Choice of battery For all the bashing about the type and size of the battery for a 7 seater car, Kia chose the tradeoff smartly. We all want bigger and bigger batteries in all the EVs in the market. But batteries takes considerable space. Providing a 70+ KWh battery might have either made the 3rd row of useless, or almost eliminated the boot, both of which would have reduced its utility as a 7 seater family MPV. They also went with NMC, which takes less space, although it's a bit more expensive. Which is again a good tradeoff, because a lakh here and there doesn't matter for a 20 lakh car, as much as practicality does. *Kia is solving a problem here, not fighting the specs sheet war with Tata and Mahindra.* If this was a 5 star rated car, I would have already booked it. I read that Kia has sent the car for Bharat NCAP, and is confident of a good score. But even as things stand, it I am tempted to book it. Here's what makes it better than other EVs for me: - 7 seater: Although I don't always need the 7 seats, it's good to have. A kid can sleep in the last row and 4 adults can sit very comfortably. - Boooot: folding the 3rd row gives a massive boot. More importantly, the boot (after flooding 3rd row) is almost cube shaped, which is more practical than the horizontal or vertical boots of most cars. I used it to the fullest in my previous Carens, to carry all sorts of items which can't be fit in most other cars (ladder, rack, plants). - The best 2nd row, period: The 2nd row can not just recline but also slide (for ref, Harrier EV does neither). Sliding it all the way back gives you enough legroom even if you are six and a half feet. Recline it, and you can have a pretty good sleep, which is impossible in any 5 seater. - 200 mm of ground clearance: Better than most EVs. (although with a long wheelbase, I would still be concerned when the car is fully loaded). Presence of features like panoramic sunroof, air purifier, ADAS, 360 degree camera etc ensure that there is no compromise on features either, so I can keep using the car for several years without feeling the itch to upgrade.

source_target: 2025 Kia Carens Clavis EV Review - Page 5

Keeping a lower capacity battery (which obviously occupies lesser space), they have been able to liberate more in-cabin and boot space. Very clever engineering, I must say Quote: Ans: Choice of battery For all the bashing about the type and size of the battery for a 7 seater car, Kia chose the tradeoff smartly. We all want bigger and bigger batteries in all the EVs in the market. But batteres takes considerable space. Quote: If this was a 5 star rated car, I would have already booked it. I read that Kia has sent the car for Bharat NCAP, and is confident of a good score. But even as things stand, it I am tempted to book it. Indeed that is one lacuna when compared to other EVs. I am also eagerly waiting for them to come out with the Bharat NCAP ratings. Also, another con for Clavis EV is that it is a FWD car. MUVs are expected to carry 6/7 passengers (along with their luggage) in comfort. Here a RWD car would have been preferred but then who knows it might have eaten up the boot space. So I am kind of OK with the trade-off Quote: Here's what makes it better than other EVs for me: - 7 seater: Although I don't always need the 7 seats, it's good to have. A kid can sleep in the last row and 4 adults can sit very comfortably. - Boooot: folding the 3rd row gives a massive boot. More importantly, the boot (after flooding 3rd row) is almost cube shaped, which is more practical than the horizontal or vertical boots of most cars. I used it to the fullest in my previous Carens, to carry all sorts of items which can't be fit in most other cars (ladder, rack, plants). - The best 2nd row, period: The 2nd row can not just recline but also slide (for ref, Harrier EV does neither). Sliding it all the way back gives you enough legroom even if you are six and a half feet. Recline it, and you can have a pretty good sleep, which is impossible in any 5 seater. I agree 100% with that. No other car be it ICE or EV offers that kind of flexibility with the second row of seats. It can slide back and forth, it can recline, it can electrically tumble fold from left side with the push of a button and it can be tumble fold manually when done from the right side. Quote: - 200 mm of ground clearance: Better than most EVs. (although with a long wheelbase, I would still be concerned when the car is fully loaded). Presence of features like panoramic sunroof, air purifier, ADAS, 360 degree camera etc ensure that there is no compromise on features either, so I can keep using the car for several years without feeling the itch to upgrade. All the features listed above are kind of must haves in today's cars. One can debate on the utility of ADAS & panoramic sunroof but ample ground clearance, 360 degree cameras, rear-AC vents etc are non-negotiable

text: Quote: Originally Posted by VindyWheels Can an EV be charged in small resorts or homestays in hill stations without causing overload issues? I read that you should not keep using the fast chargers at the charging outlets more than 2-3 times in a row, and you have to do slow charging every 3rd cycle. Yes, and you can adjust the Amps that can be pulled by the car on the charger, based on the outlet or load limits. If they have a 16A socket, you can charge at the max 12A (2.8 kW), or if they only have small outlets, or if you need to use an extension, you can trickle charge at 6A (1.4 kW). I carry an extension board and 16A to 6A converter plug in my frunk, when I plan on destination charging at such locations. Quote: Originally Posted by pseudo_coder Just read that Android auto and Apple carplay are wired! Is that right? Why?? Yes, all Hyundai vehicles with the Gen5W system only have wired AA/CarPlay. People speculated it is because of the BlueLink/SIM support taking up slot for WiFi, but it might just be licensing issues. You can purchase a USB adapter for 2-3k to enable wireless support, plenty of adapters on Amazon.

source_target: 2025 Kia Carens Clavis EV Review - Page 5
text: Hi Did the TD today and loved the car. Clavis EV has two battery packs, 42 kWh (404km) and 51.4 kWh (490km). The price difference is around 2 lacs. Does the additional 86km range make much of a difference practically? I mean, in the city/hometown, where you are 90% of the time, it doesn't matter at all, and on the highway for a 5-6 hour drive, anyway, you have to recharge during the lunch break. So, is the bigger battery pack worth the 2 lacs? My TD today was on the top variant; the SA is unsure if the higher power output from the larger battery pack will make any difference in the driving mechanics. Cheers VW

source_target: 2025 Kia Carens Clavis EV Review - Page 5
text: Quote: Originally Posted by VindyWheels Does the additional 86km range make much of a difference practically? I mean, in the city/hometown, where you are 90% of the time, it doesn't matter at all, and on the highway for a 5-6 hour drive, anyway, you have to recharge during the lunch break. So, is the bigger battery pack worth the 2 lacs? I'd say it's more about peace of mind, not about the 86 kms. Suppose you are on the highway and your primary planned charger isn't working for whatever reason, that extra 10Kw could be the difference between being able to search for more chargers or getting stranded. I'd always recommend anyone looking at an EV to go for the biggest battery they can afford.
