text: Quote: Originally Posted by adithya.m.bhat Comparing the two Electrics At the end of the drive, the BE6 averaged 8.2 km/kWh and the Ioniq 5 managed 7.2km/kWh. For those curious about the high energy efficiency of both vehicles, the route itself is a downslope. We descend almost 1100m from the western ghat mountains to the sea level at Mangalore. The Ioniq’s lower efficiency is likely due to different regen settings. Not a conclusive comparison — maybe I’ll do a proper test sometime. Thank you for this somewhat apples to apples comparison. Should put to bed several myths like - NMC batteries are inherently more efficient than LFP batteries because of the chemistry, Hyundai vehicles are inherently more efficient than other or that Hyundai has some super efficient BMS. There is only one company who are markedly more efficient than the competition - Tesla and even that is a combination of a better BMS, a more aerodynamic design, a very efficient motor which was designed in-house and also having a smart chassis design which has allowed them to reduce weight while maintaining safety (The Model Y juniper LR dual motor is 1997kg according to the Tesla website - this is 10-20% lighter than the BE6, XEV9e and the Harrier.EV).

source_target: Adithya's Mahindra BE6 P3 Ownership Review + Log - Page 5
text: Quote: Originally Posted by Rossiter Nice writeup and the car looks stunning! Just wanted to comment on the charging at Sira and Hassan. The BE6/XEV are 400V cars, which means on these two chargers it can take a maximum of ~135kW, the guns being rated for 300A. 800V cars like the Ioniq/EV6 can max out at 200kW in the same charger with 300A guns. At the Hassan charger, going by the photo you posted from the screen, if you look at the bottom, the info shows that the car is demanding 115A, which the charger is providing, which is roughly 50kW at your car's voltage. That said, this is not normal, the demand from the car should've been a lot more. Thanks! Based on the info in post #8, I observed that the car can request up to 465 V. If we assume the advertised peak charging power of 175 kW, that would imply a peak current draw of: 175,000 W ÷ 465 V ≈ 376.36 A. So it looks like the car is designed to draw around 376 A at most. Of course, actual current draw could be limited by various factors—thermal limits, SoC, or BMS-controlled constraints depending on conditions like battery/cooling system temps. But from my end, there's no way to see what the bottleneck is. All I can observe is that the current demand is lower than peak in most real-world scenarios. Any suggestions on how I can dig deeper into this? I’m tempted to head to the ChargeZone 360 kW station near Vellore and test if I can finally hit the car’s upper limit on a properly high-capacity charger! Also, does anyone know of any charger where the BE6 has actually hit or come close to its advertised 175 kW peak in practice?

source_target: Adithya's Mahindra BE6 P3 Ownership Review + Log - Page 5
text: Quote: Originally Posted by adithya.m.bhat Of course, actual current draw could be limited by various factors—thermal limits, SoC, or BMS-controlled constraints depending on conditions like battery/cooling system temps. Power draw from charger is dependent on voltage of the battery pack being charged. Two 400V EVs connected to two guns of a shared charger of rating 200A and 200KW, will pull about 100A each, that would be around 47KW power each, assuming charging voltage of 470V. So even though charger supports 200KW, in this case only 94KW total output is achieved. On the same charger, two 800V EVs will also pull 100A each but at a higher voltage of 930V (assumed charging voltage for the pack), that would be 93KW each. So total 186KW is delivered from the charger. In your case, if there was a Nexon charging on the same charger and pulling 30KW then it was  drawing about 80A current already. Nexon battery pack is 320V so charging voltage would be around 380V. This means that the zeon charger supported 200A current in total (80-85A to nexon and 125 to your BE6) even though it is capable for 200KW. Max power from that charger will be achieved when an 800V EV is getting charged.

source_target: Adithya's Mahindra BE6 P3 Ownership Review + Log - Page 5
text: Quote: Originally Posted by adithya.m.bhat More Exploration of BE6 It was a Thursday and I was all set for a trip to my ancestral home near Kalasa in the Chikkamagalur district. I was quite looking forward to this drive — not just for the destination, but because this time I planned to check out the (new for me) 200kW Zeon charger and see how my car handles peak charging power. Shoutout to shyampsunder for tipping me off about this location. Since it was a weekday, I hoped that the station wouldn’t be crowded — which meant a better chance of getting the full 200kW all for myself. I was particularly excited to see if I could experience the 175kW+ charging firsthand. The drive till Hassan only added to my optimism — long, open stretches post-Nelamangala and empty toll booths. BE6 was in its element, munching miles effortlessly. I reached the Zeon charger around 4:20 PM. This station had two 200kW guns and four 160kW guns. But the first disappointment hit — a Tata Nexon had just arrived ahead of me and was prepping to plug in. I began working on a Plan B: skip the experiment and charge just enough to comfortably reach my destination. But a quick glance at my SoC changed that plan fast—36%, thanks to a bit of enthusiastic right-foot driving. I could make it, theoretically, but the margin would be razor thin. Plus, I needed enough buffer for the return trip as well. So, I plugged in. Before initiating, the charger screen showed 150kW (800V) and 80kW (400V). But once charging started, the power climbed to just 50kW and stayed there. A bit underwhelming. Quote: Originally Posted by Rossiter Nice writeup and the car looks stunning! Just wanted to comment on the charging at Sira and Hassan. The BE6/XEV  are 400V cars, which means on these two chargers it can take a maximum  of ~135kW, the guns being rated for 300A. 800V cars like the Ioniq/EV6  can max out at 200kW in the same charger with 300A guns. At the Hassan charger, going by the photo you posted from the screen, if  you look at the bottom, the info shows that the car is demanding 115A,  which the charger is providing, which is roughly 50kW at your car's  voltage. That said, this is not normal, the demand from the car  should've been a lot more. Quote: Originally Posted by adithya.m.bhat Thanks! Based on the info in post #8, I observed that the car can request up to 465 V. If we assume the advertised peak charging power of 175 kW, that would imply a peak current draw of: 175,000 W ÷ 465 V ≈ 376.36 A. So it looks like the car is designed to draw around 376 A at most. Of course, actual current draw could be limited by various factors—thermal limits, SoC, or BMS-controlled constraints depending on conditions like battery/cooling system temps. But from my end, there's no way to see what the bottleneck is. All I can observe is that the current demand is lower than peak in most real-world scenarios. Any suggestions on how I can dig deeper into this? I’m tempted to head to the ChargeZone 360 kW station near Vellore and test if I can finally hit the car’s upper limit on a properly high-capacity charger! Also, does anyone know of any charger where the BE6 has actually hit or come close to its advertised 175 kW peak in practice? In many cases, it is not the car's current capacity that is the limiting factor but the current capacity of the equipment of the DC fast charger. Passing high currents through a conductor causes a lot of heat to be generated (heat increases proportional to the square of the current). So this thermal issue limits the maximum current that most chargers can output. For very high currents (above 200A), you need either thicker cables, shorter cables, active cable cooling or a combination of them all to keep the heat under control so these chargers are very rare - cost and usability both take turn for the worse with the above solutions. Now in the case you outlined in your post, the charger at Hasan is rated at 150kW for 800V charging and 80kW for 400V charging. That means that the maximum current that the fast charger can output is around 200A, irrespective of the voltage. The BE6 is built on a ~400V architecture (435V as per the photo) so when connected to a 200A DC charger, it can charge at a maximum rate of ~85kW. In your case, there was also a Nexon (another 400V car) charging at 30kW so it was using ~75A of the available current capacity so your BE6 could only draw a current of around ~125A(It was actually drawing 115A which is still pretty close to the max). So the speeds you observed do make sense. Now the 360kW fast charger that you are planning to visit should be able to provide full power to your BE6. The 360kW rating is for 800V (i think) so the maximum current the charger can supply is 450A. Your BE6 should be able to charge at full speed on it. Do let us know of the results if you do try it out. As for your question about if anybody has seen the BE6 charge at its max rate, there are some posts on the XEV 9e thread where the XEV is shown to charge at a peak rate of ~180kW (and maintain that rate for a surprisingly long time). Since the hardware is the same, the BE should do it as well as long as the charger support it.

text: Quote: Originally Posted by abhishekjoshi84 Power draw from charger is dependent on voltage of the battery pack being charged. ...This means that the zeon charger supported 200A current in total... Quote: Originally Posted by Sentau In many cases, it is not the car's current capacity that is the limiting factor but the current capacity of the equipment of the DC fast charger.... maximum current that the fast charger can output is around 200A, irrespective of the voltage.... Thanks for the detailed explanation — it really helps clear things up. It’s becoming clear that the advertised peak power of a charger only makes sense when presented alongside the corresponding voltage, and ideally, the peak current as well. Without that context, the power figure alone can be quite misleading. That said, we often see chargers labelled 30 kW or 60 kW in cities and on highways. In most real-world scenarios — assuming no power-sharing across vehicles — users do seem to receive close to the rated output. Does that suggest these chargers are capable of delivering both high voltage (400 V or even 800 V) and relatively high current (around 150 A at 400 V), but are ultimately limited by the site's sanctioned load? And since 150 A is still within the range of passively cooled cables, perhaps that’s why the same cables without any active cooling works here. Now, coming back to the bigger, high power chargers - considering how voltage and peak current directly affects charging performance, I find it surprising that most public chargers don’t clearly display all three specs — power, voltage, and current. It’s obviously information that operators have, but it rarely makes it to the end user, even though it's crucial for setting realistic expectations. Data like peak current or actual power-voltage combinations seem like ideal candidates for crowd-sourced platforms like PlugShare. Is there a reason this hasn’t caught on yet? Or are there apps that already provide this level of detail and I’ve just missed them? Quote: Originally Posted by Sentau ...there are some posts on the XEV 9e thread where the XEV is shown to charge at a peak rate of ~180kW ... Since the hardware is the same, the BE should do it as well as long as the charger support it. I looked through the apps and could only find the ultra-fast charging station at Vellore. Are there more of these around?

You can try any 400A+ capable chargers to see if it hits the maximum, but it really doesn't make too much difference time-wise. This means that the zeon charger supported 200A current in total (80-85A to nexon and 125 to your BE6) even though it is capable for 200KW. Max power from that charger will be achieved when an 800V EV is getting charged. Quote: Originally Posted by Sentau Now in the case you outlined in your post, the charger at Hasan is rated at 150kW for 800V charging and 80kW for 400V charging. That means that the maximum current that the fast charger can output is around 200A, irrespective of the voltage. The BE6 is built on a ~400V architecture (435V as per the photo) so when connected to a 200A DC charger, it can charge at a maximum rate of ~85kW. In your case, there was also a Nexon (another 400V car) charging at 30kW so it was using ~75A of the available current capacity so your BE6 could only draw a current of around ~125A(It was actually drawing 115A which is still pretty close to the max). So the speeds you observed do make sense. Now the 360kW fast charger that you are planning to visit should be able to provide full power to your BE6. The 360kW rating is for 800V (i think) so the maximum current the charger can supply is 450A. Your BE6 should be able to charge at full speed on it. Do let us know of the results if you do try it out. What both of you are saying (current limitation of chargers), is applicable to standalone chargers, like the regular 60/120kW chargers which have a finite number of rectifiers to be allotted to each vehicle connected. For 60kW, it's usually 2 rectifiers of 30kW each, and for 120kW, it's 4. Each rectifier has a max output of 80A regardless of voltage. If two cars are connected in a 60kW charger, 2 rectifiers will be allocated to each car, regardless of voltage/current demand. In some 120kW chargers, there is slightly better flexibility, wherein 3 can be allocated to 1 car based on the demand, and the remaining to the other car. This makes load-sharing a bit easier for the EV owner. In the Zeon Hassan (Sira and other stations) charger, the concept is different, wherein there are totally 8 rectifiers of 25kW each, and each can be allocated dynamically across any of the 6 guns depending on the demand from the vehicle. If the vehicle demand is more, then more rectifiers will be allocated. There is total flexibility unlike the standalone chargers. Since the vehicle demand in this case was 115A, two rectifiers were allocated, giving a total of 50kW. The Nexon charging next to the BE6 had no impact on this charging experience. It’s becoming clear that the advertised peak power of a charger only makes sense when presented alongside the corresponding voltage, and ideally, the peak current as well. Without that context, the power figure alone can be quite misleading. That said, we often see chargers labelled 30 kW or 60 kW in cities and on highways. In most real-world scenarios — assuming no power-sharing across vehicles — users do seem to receive close to the rated output. Does that suggest these chargers are capable of delivering both high voltage (400 V or even 800 V) and relatively high current (around 150 A at 400 V), but are ultimately limited by the site's sanctioned load? And since 150 A is still within the range of passively cooled cables, perhaps that’s why the same cables without any active cooling works here. Now, coming back to the bigger, high power chargers - considering how voltage and peak current directly affects charging performance, I find it surprising that most public chargers don’t clearly display all three specs — power, voltage, and current. It’s obviously information that operators have, but it rarely makes it to the end user, even though it's crucial for setting realistic expectations. Data like peak current or actual power-voltage combinations seem like ideal candidates for crowd-sourced platforms like PlugShare. Is there a reason this hasn’t caught on yet? Or are there apps that already provide this level of detail and I’ve just missed them? I looked through the apps and could only find the ultra-fast charging station at Vellore. Are there more of these around? If the data that you are mentioning (voltage, amps, peak power etc), is advertised, it'll only end up confusing the common EV user. In many cases, the owners are not aware of the peak power their vehicle can take and end up using connectors which have high amps. Which is why the onus is on the car manufacturers, media, and CPOs to educate the owners into selecting the right kind of charger/connectors for their vehicles. There are quite a few ultra-fast charging stations around. Zeon at Bagepalli, couple of stations coming up in Chitradurga and Mysore which will be at this output/amp level. But like I mentioned earlier, the difference in time between a 300A charger and 400A charger is a few minutes.

source_target: Adithya's Mahindra BE6 P3 Ownership Review + Log - Page 5
text: The Screen Guard Saga... a Clean Car… and the peak charging experience The Screen Guard Saga... Let’s just say the BE6’s twin 12-inch screens have a strong relationship with fingerprints. They seem to attract smudges faster than we can wipe them off. At this point, the number of prints might outpace my daughter’s counting skills (honestly, I’m not sure she’s even taught to count that far yet). But yeah, you get the idea. And if you don’t, here’s visual proof: Initially, I thought of going back to the dealership to get a screen protector fitted, but let’s just say my buying experience didn’t leave me jumping to revisit them. Didn’t feel like going back for something this small. So naturally, Amazon it was. In full Clarkson mode, I thought: “How hard can it be?” I mean, I’ve done phone screen guards before — twice on mine, once on my wife’s. I figured this couldn’t be that different. Clean the screen, wipe it with alcohol, slap on the protector, done. So I scrolled through listings and found one that specifically mentioned BE6 compatibility. Had about 70 reviews and a decent 4.2-star rating. Good enough for me. It even came with an option: matte vs. glossy. Glossy felt right—looked better and was a hundred bucks cheaper. Clicked Buy Now, and it showed up about a week later. Took the car out to a well-lit part of the apartment basement — important if you’re trying to avoid dust specks ruining your OCD. First, a quick unboxing: The long box had the screen guard itself. The smaller one? A surprise: an extra protector for the steering wheel screen! If only I’d known that before paying for PPF there... Inside the package, there were two screen protectors, two cleaning kits (alcohol wipe, microfiber cloth, dust stickers), one squeegee and a small but strangely confident instruction manual. Wiped down the displays with the included alcohol swab, followed by the microfiber cloth. It felt exactly like cleaning a phone screen — just on a much wider scale. Honestly, one kit was enough to get both screens spotless. According to the manual: “Now, gently peel off the sticker tagged Step-1 to expose the gummy side that will stick to your screen…” So far, so good. “Align the screen protector with the topmost edge of the screen and slowly apply using thrust of your thumb…” Thrust of your thumb? On a touchscreen? Who writes these things? Anyway, I started with the process. And quickly realised — this was no phone screen. The displays are long, the cockpit is tight, and aligning the top edge while hunched over the center console is not something I would call ergonomic. Still, managed to get it on, but noticed a visible misalignment along one edge. Not a lot, but enough to annoy me every time I looked. So I did what I’ve always done with my phone: tried to lift and reapply. Except, this thing wasn’t budging. And of course, I had clipped my nails that very morning. Two minutes of fingernail gymnastics later, I managed to lift it off without bending it. Took a breath, said a silent prayer, and gave it another go. This time, I lined up both the top and the sides. No thumb-thrusting involved. Just gentle pressure and careful patience. Miraculously, it worked. A few bubbles appeared, but the included squeegee made quick work of them. Peeled off the outer film and admired the result. The gloss is striking. With the screens OFF, it was like having mirrors. Here’s a before and after comparison: Before: After: It's been a couple of days since the install. the screens feel a bit clearer. I’m not sure if that’s the gloss or just my satisfaction talking. Either way, I’m happy. And the best part? Fingerprints are way fewer now. Even when they do appear, a quick wipe with a towel gets rid of them. Satisfying result overall. ...a Clean Car Once the screens were sorted, the rest of the car started to look neglected. Called up Rustaan from Ultimate Detailers and booked a quick clean-up session for Saturday afternoon. After a week on the road, the car had collected a fair bit of dirt and dust. A great chat and an even better clean later, I headed back home. And because the universe has a sense of humour, it rained later that evening. Not a downpour, only a light drizzle, just enough to annoy any freshly cleaned car owner. …The Ultra-Fast Charging Experience After hearing all the talk about charging speeds, peak power, current, voltage, and whatnot, it was clear—I had to visit the ultra-fast ChargeZone charger at Vellore. You know me by now, I don’t need much convincing to hit the road. Even though the XEV 9e’s charging performance is already well-documented (link here if you haven’t seen it: https://www.team-bhp.com/forum/elect...1-minutes.html (Mahindra XEV 9E charge curve shows a peak of 189 kW with 20-80% in 21 minutes) ), I wanted to see firsthand how the BE6 handled a 360kW charger. The plan was simple: Start from home Sunday morning, Reach ChargeZone with sub-20% SoC, Plug in to the 360kW charger, Charge up and head back. That’s it. No sightseeing. No family visits. Just the road, and the charger. Bangalore gave us the perfect start - mild sun, mostly cloudy, that early morning vibe. Left home with 42% SoC, which was about 10% over what I had calculated as necessary. On the way, the Mrs. wanted to take the wheel—and when the ‘home minister’ makes a request, the correct answer is always a polite yes. She doesn’t drive often—usually just highways, maybe once a month or less—so I quietly set the drive mode to Default and asked her to switch regen to L3, confident it would give her just enough punch with the right amount of deceleration control. I even suggested turning on Lane Keep Assist. That’s when it struck me—was I trusting the car more now? Or was it both the car and the Mrs.? Either way, I felt relaxed enough to dive into the snack stash with my daughter. Yes, we’re those people—the kind who n-e-e-d to munch on something the second we settle into the car. It's a tradition at this point. I did remind her about the buffer I had accounted for, but clearly, the joy of driving the car got the better of her. She was happily cruising in triple digits, overtaking anything that moved on the expressway. Once we hit the end of the highway and crossed the toll booth, we decided to switch seats, which also gave us a chance to click a few more photos of the car. From there, we moved onto two-lane roads. And again, hats off to TN roads—they somehow always feel smoother than KA roads. There was a small downhill ghat stretch where the regen added back 3–4%. Soon after, we rolled into ChargeZone with 15% SoC. There was only one car—a BMW—which I suspected was using the 350kW plug. I parked nearby, walked over, and confirmed using the ChargeZone app that the BMW was indeed in the slot I wanted: TN0015/A. But as luck would have it, just as I walked up to check, the BMW left. I ran back to move the BE6 into position of the 350kW gun. Plugged in, loaded the app, tapped Start Session, and immediately heard the charger’s coolant fans spool up like a jet. Good sign. I slid back into the driver’s seat to monitor the numbers and was instantly surprised. The display showed 183 kW!! BE6 is rated at 175 kW peak. Then came 184… 185… and it finally 190 kW somewhere between 35 % and 39 %. Mind = blown. Even though I had already seen these numbers posted on the forum for the same powertrain in XEV 9e, witnessing it firsthand was something else! That feeling of watching the numbers climb past the official spec—pure EV geek joy. But at 44%, the power suddenly dropped to 1kW and the session ended with an error. No worries. Started a second session. Power jumped right back to 185kW. After 48%, it tapered to 155kW, then around 143kW post 64%. Right then, an XEV 9e plugged in next to me. I expected load sharing to kick in and drop the power—but after briefly dipping to 101kW, it bounced back up to 144kW all within 3-5 seconds. Impressive charger! By 74%, it was down to 120kW. After 80%, charging dropped below 50kW. Once you’ve seen 190kW, anything below 50 feels like a crawl. We ended the session and walked into the adjacent CCD for a break. The two sessions took about 9 and 18 minutes each—27 minutes in total to charge from 15% to 85%. Honestly, we were so hooked on watching the kW numbers climb and fall, we barely noticed the time fly by. After our coffee break, we got back into the car and followed Maps… which led us onto what looked like a dried riverbed-turned-temporary mud road. Somehow, Google thought this was a good idea. Thankfully, it lasted only 500m and we were back on tarmac. Took a longer route home, clocked another 250km before finally parking. Final Odometer: 7339km Parting Shot: BE6- Always ready to take on the road Cheers! — BE6 Owner, proudly counting kW and screen smudges

source_target: Adithya's Mahindra BE6 P3 Ownership Review + Log - Page 5
text: Quote: Originally Posted by adithya.m.bhat Experiment 1: The Charging Speed Conclusion: This experiment confirmed what many EV owners suspect — the charging curve isn’t linear, and fast-charging doesn't stay “fast” all the way to 100%. Once you’re past 80–85%, the power drop is pretty steep, and from 90% onward it’s basically a trickle. So, if you're planning a quick top-up during a road trip, it’s smarter to unplug once you hit ~85% and get moving — the time vs gain after that just isn’t worth it. Full charges are better left to overnight AC charging at home — not something to chase at public fast chargers. Adithya, thank you for your detailed ownership review. Very well written and documented with photographs.

text: Experiment 3: The Range This has been one of my longest and most time-consuming experiments so far. It took me nearly 3 weeks and 950+km of driving to gather observations, and another 2 weeks to pen them down. I really hope this makes sense, stays relevant, and is useful to fellow enthusiasts. Whenever we talk about cars, one question inevitably pops up: " Kitna deti hai? " (Translation: " What's the mileage? ") With EVs, the analogous question I get these days is: " What's the range? " My usual answer, when I’m in the mood to indulge, is that the ARAI MIDC-claimed range is 683 km, but in real-world conditions, I have observed anywhere between 400 km and 600 km, depending on terrain and driving style. The instant I share those numbers, I can almost see the judgment in people’s eyes. It feels like they’re thinking: “ Oh, he’s struggling to admit it only gives 400 km, so he’s beating around the bush. ” No matter how much I try to explain after that, it’s pointless – I’m already branded as “ that guy boasting about his EV. ” So, this experiment is my attempt to put the “range” question to rest – once and for all. Objective : To determine the real-world range of the BE6. My first idea was to go full “engineering mode” and figure out the aerodynamic drag coefficient (CdA), rolling resistance coefficient (µr), and build an energy consumption curve based on speed. But Mahindra hasn’t officially released these numbers, and I didn’t want to rely on Google or ChatGPT guesstimates. So, I decided to figure it out myself through a coast-down test. The plan was simple: Find a long, level stretch of road. Start at, say, 80 km/h, put the transmission in 'N', and measure the time it takes to roll down to a lower speed (say, 70 km/h). By performing this test at various speeds, I could solve the force equations to estimate CdA and μr. Armed with this plan, I headed to my favourite test patch, the Bangalore–Chennai expressway, hunting for a perfectly level road. The BE6 has a built-in altimeter, and I kept an eye on it the whole time, hoping to spot a flat section. To my disappointment, the road throughout has some gradient and it wasn’t good enough for me. I really had no expectation from other roads as even if I could find a level patch, there would have been the usual traffic to interfere. So, ‘back to the drawing board‘, it was. Quote: “The best ideas come when the brain thinks of nothing.” – Me The next day, while staring at a wall (yes, I do that when inspiration fails), a better idea struck: Why not use cruise control over long distances to measure energy consumption directly from the car? The BE6 shows energy efficiency data per trip, which I could use. I needed a fixed start and end point and a road where I could maintain constant speed with cruise control. Both are easily achieved with my (now) favourite 120km speed limit, NE7 stretch. I decided to collect two data points for each test speed, one for the onward journey, one for the return. The onward journey starts from the cloverleaf flyover (next to a signboard), ends just past the toll exit of the expressway. The return journey starts after the toll (after the weighbridge), end just before the Bangalore-Kolar road exit. Admittedly, this method does not give me the details of aerodynamic drag coeff and rolling coeff directly, but it does give me what I ultimately care about : energy used at different steady speeds, which I can convert into range. The only downside is that the altitude is an extremely important factor in determining the range. The elevation reading at the clover leaf is around 2933 feet and at the other end is 2531 feet. On the return journey, the start point has an elevation of 2531feet while the end point has a slightly different altitude value of 2912 feet. By pairing both onward and return journey for each speed reading, I can partially cancel out the effect of elevation change. To ensure consistency, I kept all conditions constant. The windows were all rolled up, the AC blower and set temp were same. The Mars Roof was shut, the mobiles too were disconnected from the car and the music was played through the inbuilt amazon music integration with the same volume. The SonicSuite was off, ambient lighting set to highest brightness level, HUD was ON and the drive mode was put to ‘default’ mode. Regeneration mode didn’t matter because cruise control manages regen on its own. Once all of these were set, the trips A and B were reset to zero. This ritual became my pre-run checklist. Since the BE6 has adaptive cruise control, it decelerates if it detects any slow moving vehicle ahead in the same lane. In all the runs, I tried to avoid as much of this deceleration as possible by changing the lane in advance. A special mention to the lane change assist feature of the BE6, it does all the work with a initiation of the turn indicator and a gentle nudge on the steering wheel. Here’s a HUD grab from one such manoeuvre: Inevitably, there were a few situations where I had to reduce speed. The cruise control system automatically slows down when it detects steering input, such as while navigating a curve, and I allowed it to do so. On a few occasions, both lanes were blocked by slower vehicles, leaving me no choice but to let the cruise slow down and wait for a safe gap before overtaking. There was also a moment when a fast vehicle was approaching in the right lane while I was attempting to pass a slower vehicle on my left; although I was moving slightly faster, I wasn’t confident of completing the maneuver before the high-speed vehicle closed in. In that instance, I temporarily disengaged cruise control, waited for the vehicle to pass, and then resumed. Finally, there’s one inoperational toll booth on this route where I had to slow down completely. However, the number of instances where the cruise was interrupted and the vehicle speed was slowed down were rare and brief, and thus, they shouldn’t materially skew the data. Observations: Below is the complete dataset comprising of dates, cruise speeds, trip distances, state-of-charge readings, ambient temperatures, and computed efficiencies. Key points to note: The trip meter has finer resolution (extra decimal precision) than the lifetime odometer, so the two don’t always match. Differences can be as high as 0.9 km. For all calculations, I’ve relied on the trip meter distance. Despite using the exact same start and end markers, recorded trip distances varied—especially on the onward leg. I can believe 10–20 m of natural measurement wiggle, but not the hundreds of meters I observed. My working theory: traffic forced me into different lane usage / overtaking patterns on different days, which slightly changes the driven path length. The return runs were more consistent (lighter traffic; I could sit in the left lane longer), whereas the onward runs involved more lane changes, explaining the scatter. The elevation readings at the four polled locations remained stable across runs. Net change: roughly 402 ft drop on the onward leg and 381 ft climb on the return. This directional gradient shows up clearly in the energy numbers later. The ambient temperature Ranged from 23°C to 32°C, with Bangalore’s weather keeping things mostly stable. Only one day (5th July, early morning) had a 23°C reading. Calculations & Results: Lets dive to the energy side of things. Mahindra states the BE6’s battery capacity as 79 kWh, but it isn’t clear to me, whether this refers to the installed or usable capacity. For those new to EVs and car batteries, it’s important to know that not all of the battery’s energy is accessible. The installed capacity represents the total energy the battery can theoretically store, while a portion is reserved as buffers, both at the lower end (to prevent deep discharge damage) and the upper end (to avoid overcharging). The energy between these thresholds is the usable capacity, typically represented as the full 0–100% SoC range you see on the dashboard. The first step, therefore, is to figure out the usable capacity of battery and check if this itself is 79kWh. In order to calculate the usable capacity, I used each trip distance and the average efficiency along with the difference of SoC observed. Usable Capacity (kWh) = Energy consumed (kWh) / (SoC Change (%) / 100) where, Energy consumed (kWh) = Trip Distance (km) / Average Efficiency (km/kWh) Applying this formula to all trips, I compiled the following computation table: Note that the y-axis on the below chart starts at 65 kWh, which exaggerates the variation in column heights for the small differences. The average usable capacity of the battery from the computations, turn out to be 75.18kWh ! That means the remaining ~4kWh is reserved as a buffer. In other words, folks at Mahindra have extracted ~95.16% as usable energy from the installed capacity of 79kWh. Impressive!!! Next, moving on to the SoC consumption and the average efficiency numbers for each trip. Below are the plots of SoC difference ordered for each run of the cruise speed. The blue columns represent the onward journey and the red columns represent the return journey. The return legs consistently show higher SoC consumption due to the 381 ft elevation climb, compared to the 402 ft descent on the onward runs. To better visualize energy use, here is the average efficiency numbers for the onward (blue) and return (red). Additionally, I have put the average of onward and return in yellow columns. The yellow line crossing through the plot is the trendline (exponential curve) for the yellow columns. This again confirms the pattern, slower the speed, better the energy efficiency and more the climb in altitude, higher energy is consumed. What stands out is that at 120 km/h, efficiency is just under half of what it is at 60 km/h. The exponential curve had the highest R² fit, so I used its equation for the next calculations. Using the trendline equation, I estimated the range for the 75.18 kWh usable capacity. Once I did calculate, I started to wonder if it even made sense as majority of people would not use the full 100% of battery anyway. From my experience alone, I tend to stop charging once I hit around 80% and plan my next charging stop coinciding with battery SoC somewhere around 10%. This way, I get the fastest charging possible without any charge power limitation while simultaneously maintaining a buffer of 10% just in case my planned charger isn’t available and I need to go to the backup one. So, with this behaviour has a baseline, I recalculated the range values corresponding to a battery discharge from 80% to 10%. The 70% of depth of discharge roughly corresponds to 52.62kWh. Here’s the range chart for both full and 70% SoC windows. Warning: The numbers below 60 and above 120 are extrapolated numbers. These could vary in real conditions. The BE6 appears capable of exceeding its claimed 683 km range on a single charge (hypermilers, where are you? 1000 km within single charge might be within reach!). Further, the 683km range corelates to an average speed somewhere between 65 and 70kmph. Third, even at the max legal speed of 120kmph, the car seems capable of travelling close to 360km in full charge! All exciting numbers. For my 70% battery discharge scenario, the numbers are equally impressive: 530+km for average cruise speed of 60kmph, 400+km for 80kmph, 315km for 100kmph and 250+km for 120kmph! All while considering only 70% of battery energy and mind you.. these are with AC and music running for the entire time. I then calculated how long the 70% battery window lasts at different speeds: This chart really helps me explain the practicality of the big battery in this vehicle. From this chart, roughly, I know the range that I would get for the set cruise speed, and further the time in which ill need to stop for a charge. Using such graph, I could figure out the desired cruise speed to time it correctly to the mealtime / restroom breaks. Driving at 120kmph? Prepare to stop roughly after 2hrs. Deciding to drive at 80kmph? You will comfortably run for 400kms and you need not stop for 5hrs! Of course, by no means is this chart, a replacement for the apps and tools aiding for energy consumption calculation and charger planning. I have collated the results of my run and extrapolated them just to know the vehicle a bit better. Conclusion: This is the kind of experiment almost no one will bother doing: it eats time, needs discipline, and demands running ~140 km test loops (repeated… and repeated) just to get clean, comparable data. But the payoff is huge: the BE6’s usable battery comes out to ~75.18 kWh of the stated 79 kWh pack (~95% accessible!), and the car can beat its official 683 km claim when driven efficiently . Even in a realistic usage window (my 80%→10% routine) it still serves up serious highway legs. At the other extreme, hold a steady 120 km/h and you’re still good for 250+ km before you need to stop , more if you’re willing to use the full pack. That’s proper long‑distance usability. That said, the numbers may not be same for all. The wind direction and speed are unaccounted as there was no measurable way to read it. I had a great time doing this experiment: geeky, time-consuming, totally worth it. If you’ve got ideas on what I should try next, throw them in. Current Odometer reading: 8567km Stay charged! — BE6 Owner, obsessively tracking kWh like it’s a sport

text: Happy to report this morning the "Rosso Diablo" crossed 10,000 miles (16,000kms) in 11-months of ownership. After living with it for this substantial time-period, there are many things I learned about this absolutely amazing car. Will share a 10,000 mile ownership report soon. 10,000 Miles...Yayyy A closer look to understand the math of range/mileage. If you see my average Kilo Watt hour (KWH) is 296/KWH. I have been driving a bit sanely lately and brought it down from an insanely ridiculous 340kwh that I was averaging. Let me explain what it means. From the Tesla forums, this is the general thumb rule of the mileage/range you get if you are in this range 190kwh-220kwh- 5+ miles per kWh which is considered excellent. 220kwh-260kwh- 4 miles -4.9 miles per kWh which is considered good. 261kwh-290kwh- 3 miles-3.9 miles per kWh which is considered average. To put this in perspective. If you see the picture below, my average is 296kwh. This means that I have averaged roughly 3 miles per 1% of battery. If the general usable range is 80%-20% which is 60% : 60X 3 Miles : 180 miles/288 kms for every charge. Heck, who wants mileage from an EV. I need to get back to my previous blinding acceleration runs.

source_target: 2023 Tesla Model 3 Performance (M3P) | 29,000 miles (47,000 kms) | Ownership Report. EDIT: Sold - Page 7
text: 2023 Tesla Model 3 Performance-10,000 Miles Report Whew! Finally, got sometime to pen down a brief report of how I feel about the Rosso Diablo after living with it for nearly 1- year now. I hit 10,000 miles/16,000 kms earlier this week in roughly about 11-months of ownership. As most of you know, I work 100% from home with a little bit of travel thrown in, so these miles like the BMW miles are purely pleasure miles. It’s a surprise to us as well how the 10K miles came up so quickly despite not doing any long trip with the Tesla till date (Barring a couple of visits to Vancouver, Canada which isn’t a long trip in my books). After living with it for 10K miles, its time to pore over again on some of Tesla Model 3 Performance’s key parameters : Acceleration/Performance Nobody including me can start a feedback of a Tesla without talking about the acceleration which is “intoxicating” to say the least. If I can roughly think how much time I spent between the 2 modes “ Chill” and “ Sports”. I can it’s about 40%-Sports and 60%-Chill. After the recent software update, this toggle between modes happens on the fly and while driving via. the scroll wheel on the steering wheel. Chill mode itself is plenty fast but, when you toggle to sport, the acceleration becomes indescribable as acceleration becomes savage and instantaneous and super car levels. Many people other than my family sat in my Tesla and I haven't had a passenger yet that hasn't reacted. My wife and mom hate the acceleration as it makes them giddy, nauseous and alarmed. But, everyone else (including the small kid-Your’s truly) always squeals with delight. Just to put things in perspective the two models roughly translate to the following : Chill Mode- This is plenty fast and if I can roughly equate the acceleration, it can do 0-60mph in 4.5 seconds. Sports Mode- This I call it the  “Flying” mode and does it in 3.1 seconds and this mode can also be termed as a Lamborghini or any other super car killer. When you activate this mode, there is a major difference in power from Chill to Sports. There is nothing around you on the road including M cars that can accelerate the way this car does. In Chill mode, you need to gently press the accelerator to reach unmentionable speeds quickly. In Sports mode, you need to be careful “not” to press the accelerator by mistake. If you know what I mean, even the slightest input the M3P rockets forward with blinding surge that makes you feel that you are “whooshing” away into space. This is the single most fun factor of owning the Tesla. Steering & Handling Honestly, the steering doesn’t feel as good as the BMW to hold (no protruding leather, thinner curves) but, what it lacks in the steering feel, it makes up very well with the actually feel of the car. The steering is spot on and is super sharp to inputs. A slight twitch and you know the exact direction it will go. There’s also so much traction to work with. It holds on through corners astonishingly well. I regularly throw this car into roundabouts (4-exits) at unmentionable speeds and it sticks to its course like a leech. You can set up where the power goes – RWD is possible for drifting – and this does help enjoying the car more as it slides and still gives you the feeling of control. With a balanced set up, it’s a confidence inspiring drive. I haven’t played with the track options yet as saving it for a track day that I plan to do in future. An analogy I would like to repeat that I mentioned in my ownership report - When you are driving on freeways with 4 or 6 lanes and want to change lanes at high speed (70mph), it’s a visual treat. You can see it slide like an eel from one lane to another so quickly and remaining so flat to the ground. It’s surreal. The handling of Tesla Model 3 Performance is like a sports car. The availability of all that breathtaking power, knowing that it will be delivered instantly, brings a smug feeling at each red light/every corner and every sweeping bend that you see in your sight. You know for sure that it will beat anything off the line. Build Quality The build quality is definitely inferior to my BMW. But, it does not feel bad in any way. There are no squeaks, no rattles or disturbing sounds from anywhere in the cabin. I’m extremely particular about any sound emanating for the cabin and it disturbs my driving experience and I just cannot tolerate it. There is no sound to disturb my Tesla driving experience. The only issue I have is when the road surface isn’t good, due to the cabin being so silent, the tire noise seeps into the cabin and that’s kind of disturbing to some extent. But, I have a solution, I switch on music and it’s gone. Well, in any case, I never drive the car without music so this downside is ignorable. Only times music is not important is when I am in my BMW and want to enjoy the exhaust note. Perhaps, if I change to a Michelin in future and move down a size from current sporty 20” low profile tires this issue will be better. But, I have no plans as the current tires are phenomenal from a performance perspective. Autopilot This took me a few months to develop the confidence. But, once you start using it. You will realize it’s better than human beings. I can make a mistake of not paying attention and sliding into my neighboring lane on a freeway but, if I switch on AUTOPILOT, there is no way the car is going to make any mistakes. It uses all of its 9-camera prowess to stick to the lane and auto-steers beautifully. In my 10,000 miles ownership, I experienced Phantom braking only ONCE. It was when I switched on the AUTOPILOT at a higher speed > 75mph and it did give me a big scare as the car assumed that another car is changing lane and coming in front of my car (while in reality the other car was very close to the lane and Tesla assumed its moving over to my lane). Phantom braking is when the Tesla brakes abruptly and with a sudden jerk scaring everyone in the car. It used to be an issue in the past but, continuous software updates have almost got rid of this issue. Not sure how much of the object recognition is neural-network-based versus discrete programming. Apart from that sole scary incident, it works beautifully. I started with day time then I developed confidence to switch on at night time. It works flawlessly well even at night time on freeways with 70+ mph speeds. The only issue is Tesla has now updated the software to ensure that you touch the wheel every 60 seconds or so (There is a camera inside the cabin that is also watching if you are sleeping or doing something different while driving)- I reckon it’s required as tons of people have misused this feature to do stupid things and jeopardizing other people’s lives on the road. Overall, it’s like an advanced version of cruise control and works well to give you a smooth, tireless drive during those long freeway drives. Charging In my entire ownership experience, I have charged my Tesla only 2-times at Superchargers and both times in Vancouver, Canada. I normally charge the car overnight at home and it’s fresh and ready to rock ’n’ roll the next morning whenever, I want some electric performance fun. My M3P comes with a 315 miles (500kms ) range. I never charge the car beyond 80% of its battery (as suggested for long battery life) and never wait beyond 20% to resume charging. In a few instances, charged to 90% and let it drop to 10% when doing the Canada trips. But, otherwise by thumb rule, the usable range is 60% (from 80% charge to 20% charge) and I get about 140 miles ( 225 kms) to 160 miles ( 250 kms) range depending on how I drive. Well, it’s usually the lower range as I didn’t buy this car for range. I bought it to have some fun. Both times at superchargers, the charging from 20% to 80% took about 35-minutes (while we are playing karaoke or watching Netflix or YouTube in the car) which helps in passing time. As I live in the west coast, the charging network here is phenomenal and there are supercharges every 15-30 miles distance so that helps. I use more of Tesla and less of BMW these days for reasons below 1. Same amount of miles (350 miles) with a BMW vs. Tesla  is $85 to $10. It’s a no-brainer which car to use for run-of the mill and daily chores. 2. Tesla is way faster than my BMW X3M40i. 3. It feels so much easier to drive the Tesla vs. BMW- Just walk in and start driving and it’s like a toy on wheels. Tires Pirelli 20” shod on the Uberturbine wheels have exceptional grip in summer, fall and rain. But, they are absolutely terrible in snow conditions as last winter, I had a couple of close calls when the car could not stop in time when braked hard (it was a snow blizzard so I can’t blame the summer performance tires). Comfort is absolutely fine as long as you don’t run into really terrible patch of road. Thankfully, 90% of Washington state has amazing road surface. 10K miles completed with no punctures till date (touchwood) and there is no uneven tire wear as well. Seats Why am I talking about seats? Well, I never paid attention to seat comfort in most of my cars but, Tesla makes you think about them. These seats have been designed and made for large bottomed Americans. It’s like sitting on some high quality leather couch. The comfort is phenomenal. This Vegan leather is super soft, extremely comfortable and has 12-way seat position adjustment including support to lumbar. Hands down this is the most comfortable seats that I ever sat in a car. BMW seats in comparison has higher quality leather which is harder, contoured at the edges like racing seats and they sometimes bites into your thighs (But, not anymore as after getting used to, it does not and feels reasonably comfortable). BMW vs. Tesla Seat Comfort Comparison- Hands Down Tesla is way more comfortable. !!! Cost of Driving 10,000 miles/16,000 kms As you can see, I spent a meagre $372 for charging my Tesla for driving it for 10,000 miles. This is unimaginable savings. To give you a perspective of the savings, a simple comparative with my BMW for driving similar miles. BMW would need approximately 29 tankfuls of gas assuming each tankful is roughly 350 miles. Each tankful of gas costs an average of $80. 29 Tankfuls X $80 : $2,280 Darn- I saved nearly $1500 by driving a Tesla. Isn’t this unreal? No wonder whoever drives a Tesla is ditching gas cars. But, I ain’t one of them (at least, for a while till I tick off my bucket lists of a couple of other gas car ownerships). So for the foreseeable future, I would love to think of Tesla as 2nd car that is equally fun as a gas car but, with lower total cost of ownership (barring the high cost of procurement….LOL) This happened yesterday. Needed 56kwh to charge from 12% battery to 80% battery. Means this one charge costed me just $5.60 for driving about 200 miles. Headlights Tesla’s headlights are also possible the brightest that I ever saw in any car till date. Due to its software prowess the adaptive lighting system is brilliant as it moves from “low beam to high beam and back to low beam” in split seconds. If I compare that to my BMW which also has the adaptive system but, it’s not as fast as the Tesla and it does not change also each time the situation arises which is possibly a bug in BMW system (dark patches on road when the lights are supposed to automatically go on to HIGH BEAM) How it works- Car turns on HIGH BEAM when there are no cars on a dark 2-lane road. However, it recognizes headlights as far as a mile or 0.5 mile ( I don’t know the exact distance but, the approaching car would be pretty far) and then it immediately moves back to LOW BEAM. Secondly, the spread of Tesla lights is excellent. If driving at night in backroads, it covers both lanes and also illuminates the shrubs/trees on either side. Lastly, I get a lot of hate from people at night. Due to the extreme bright lights, people think I am on high beam and they give me the “HATE” by turning up their HIGH BEAM. Unfortunately, they don’t realize that I am in automatic mode and in low beam and that itself probably becomes unbearable to them when they are approaching me on the road from the opposite direction. I am sure, Tesla owners may have faced this situation at night on dark patches. Notifications & Software Updates There are regular notifications on the phone (be it if you leave the windows open when you walk away from the car to software updates to charging start and stop etc…). Therefore, I scheduled it as a summary or I would go crazy with the amount it sends to the phone. Software update happens regularly and I would peg the average of once in 2 weeks at least. Downsides : Finally, let’s talk about what I don’t like or rather I miss when driving a Tesla 1. Biggest miss has to be exhaust. That’s one of the biggest fun factor in my BMW. 2. NVH is not the best even with double pane windows. The cabin is silent but, due to this silence the tire noise seeps in which is disturbing at times. I never drive without my music system not being switched on. So this hides that tire noise. 3. My performance model comes with Performance tires which perform incredible well during dry weather and rains but, these tires are a death certificate in winters as the car just does not stop in time when braking during winters. Wish they sold this car with All Season Tires. Many people have 2 sets of tires here in US but, I don’t like that arrangement. All seasons would have been nice 4. Another big irritant is the “Automatic Wipers” there are 4 levels for the wipers to be turned on apart from “ Auto”. The auto wiping is always faster than the rain falls and is not in sync with the rain pattern. Living in a place where rain is more than other places, this is an issue that I struggle with. I use the 1, 2, 3 or 4 options but, it means I am constantly using it manually to adjust based on the intensity of rain. 5. Finally, the range will have a huge variation depending on how you drive. Mine being a performance, it's difficult to drive it slow and that including the Ubertine wheels and 20" size tires take a toll on the range. I don't think my car can go beyond 250 miles 0%-100% charge especially with my driving style against the claim of 315 miles by Tesla. Summary Finally, I found a car that I can live with for long term and never get bored due to aforementioned reasons. With its battery capacity life being 300,000 miles-500,000 miles. I think this car can be used for next 25 years without even coming close to it’s EOL (End-of-Life). Thanks for reading and hope you enjoyed the feedback. Look forward to hearing back from folks who own a Tesla and also who don't about what they feel about this amazing product? Here are some latest pictures of my beautiful " Rosso Diablo" taking with my iPhone. Please click to see the full picture. All these pictures were taken yesterday (Oct 4th, 2023) and belong around my home community and neighborhood homes. Do check out the videos as well. source_target: 2023 Tesla Model 3 Performance (M3P) | 29,000 miles (47,000 kms) | Ownership Report. EDIT: Sold - Page 7
text: Just to add some points from my perspective (2023 M3P with 6000 miles in about 5 months) Quote: Originally Posted by mobike008 2023 Tesla Model 3 Performance-10,000 Miles Report Acceleration/Performance Nobody including me can start a feedback of a Tesla without talking about the acceleration which is “intoxicating” to say the least. If I can roughly think how much time I spent between the 2 modes “ Chill” and “ Sports”. I can it’s about 40%-Sports and 60%-Chill. Many people other than my family sat in my Tesla and I haven't had a passenger yet that hasn't reacted. My wife and mom hate the acceleration as it makes them giddy, nauseous and alarmed. But, everyone else (including the small kid-Your’s truly) always squeals with delight. Exactly, my feeling in my M3P. I have a friend whom I like to annoy with the sudden burst of acceleration. He tolerates me! Others simply get scared, so I drive in Chill mode whenever I have someone else in the car, and if running errands around town. It's crazy how quickly you can break all speed limits without knowing. Keeping myself in check now that the new-ness of the car has wore off. Quote: In Sports mode, you need to be careful “not” to press the accelerator by mistake. If you know what I mean, even the slightest input the M3P rockets forward with blinding surge that makes you feel that you are “whooshing” away into space. This is the single most fun factor of owning the Tesla. I've also had a scare where I pressed the accelerator instead of the brake once. The way it lurches. Took me a few mins to calm down. But apart from that, it's just addictive! Quote: Steering & Handling The steering is spot on and is super sharp to inputs. A slight twitch and you know the exact direction it will go. There’s also so much traction to work with. It holds on through corners astonishingly well. I regularly throw this car into roundabouts (4-exits) at unmentionable speeds and it sticks to its course like a leech. Quote: An analogy I would like to repeat that I mentioned in my ownership report - When you are driving on freeways with 4 or 6 lanes and want to change lanes at high speed (70mph), it’s a visual treat. You can see it slide like an eel from one lane to another so quickly and remaining so flat to the ground. It’s surreal. Aaah. Fast turns are so fun in this car! Friend has a Mustang with performance upgrades, which used to feel amazing, and now feels like a truck! And I love observing cars that change lanes like that on roadtrips. Model S looks great. BMW 5 series in black, I don't know what is it about that car, but it looks phenomenal in such scenarios. Quote: Autopilot In my 10,000 miles ownership, I experienced Phantom braking only ONCE. It was when I switched on the AUTOPILOT at a higher speed > 75mph and it did give me a big scare as the car assumed that another car is changing lane and coming in front of my car (while in reality the other car was very close to the lane and Tesla assumed its moving over to my lane). Phantom braking is when the Tesla brakes abruptly and with a sudden jerk scaring everyone in the car. It used to be an issue in the past but, continuous software updates have almost got rid of this issue. There's one corner on a specific road where my car always phantom brakes, with not a soul in sight. I don't know why. Otherwise it's mostly fine. But it is way too scared of cars that come in it's at a 90 degree angle (thinking a car turning in front of you from the opposite side). It freaks out and slams on the brakes way too early. I just disengage the autopilot proactively when I see something like that about to happen. Quote: Charging Well, it’s usually the lower range as I didn’t buy this car for range. I bought it to have some fun. . . . Both times at superchargers, the charging from 20% to 80% took about 35-minutes (while we are playing karaoke or watching Netflix or YouTube in the car) which helps in passing time. I go by the same principle. I don't think I've ever managed 300+ on a charge. More like 250. But I do think driven sensibly, it can very well reach those numbers. Quote: Tires Pirelli 20” shod on the Uberturbine wheels have exceptional grip in summer, fall and rain. But, they are absolutely terrible in snow conditions as last winter, I had a couple of close calls when the car could not stop in time when braked hard (it was a snow blizzard so I can’t blame the summer performance tires). Quote: 3. My performance model comes with Performance tires which perform incredible well during dry weather and rains but, these tires are a death certificate in winters as the car just does not stop in time when braking during winters. Wish they sold this car with All Season Tires. Many people have 2 sets of tires here in US but, I don’t like that arrangement. All seasons would have been nice I had a puncture in the first month! But has been fine since then. Agree to summer/fall performance. But I am surprised that you don't have winter/all-seasons for the colder months? This is my first car in the US and hence the first cold season with one. I live in NJ, where it doesn't snow as much, but definitely gets pretty cold. Is almost under freezing temps for 2 months. And from all that I have read, summer tires absolutely useless and turn to hard plastics and aren't recommended under 45F. I am contemplating getting a set. Just confused if I should get a set of smaller wheel+tire (18-19s) combo. Or mount new tires on the 20 Uberturbines. Also should I get winters or all seasons!?  Mounting winters would mean no road trip to the warmer states until remount the summers in March/April, else I'll wear those winters off like wax. All seasons would mean no trips up north in winter, and I really wanted to go to Quebec for Christmas. Decisions, decisions! Quote: Seats BMW vs. Tesla Seat Comfort Comparison- Hands Down Tesla is way more comfortable. !!! Have only ever taken test rides of BMWs and other German cars. Always found them quite hard. Model 3's are great. Can vouch for them. ~500 miles to Toronto in about 9 hours. Was the least tired of any cars that I've driven that kind of distance. Quote: Cost of Driving 10,000 miles/16,000 kms As you can see, I spent a meagre $372 for charging my Tesla for driving it for 10,000 miles. This is unimaginable savings. Attachment 2513080 Here's my mixed usage report. Have charged it in variety of places. I live in an apartment, so no home charging for me. Home in the image represent's the town's L2 charger (200V-30A) near my apartment. Work has L2 chargers ranging from  32A to 48A (210V). 48's have charged my car from 3% to 98% in 6-8 hours, I think. Superchargers on road trips. And the others being shopping malls. Quote: Headlights Tesla’s headlights are also possible the brightest that I ever saw in any car till date. Due to its software prowess the adaptive lighting system is brilliant as it moves from “low beam to high beam and back to low beam” in split seconds. The best I have ever used in ANY car. Bear in mind, that I haven't driven many fancy cars with LASER and other super large matrix headlamps (Audi comes to mind). Quote: Summary Finally, I found a car that I can live with for long term and never get bored due to aforementioned reasons. With its battery capacity life being 300,000 miles-500,000 miles. I think this car can be used for next 25 years without even coming close to it’s EOL (End-of-Life). I initially bought car thinking I'll keep it until just before the end of warranty. Probably replace it with another M3P, but with latest updates. Having seen the no-stalks Highland model, I might keep this longer. But then an important thing that's changing is the upcoming NACS port on pretty much all other American brands. If BMW/Porsche also start using NACS and Tesla superchargers too, I might consider them as well when the time comes. Well, the cost will also play a factor. But I like wishful thinking

text: Sorry for late reply as I was travelling entire last week on work & pleasure. Congratulations Pratik-Nice to see another Tesla Model 3 Performance owner here. They give a sense of roominess and elegance to the interiors. Others simply get scared, so I drive in Chill mode whenever I have someone else in the car, and if running errands around town. It's crazy how quickly you can break all speed limits without knowing. Keeping myself in check now that the new-ness of the car has wore off. Unfortunately, despite nearly 1-year. I just cannot keep a check on myself. I still do unmentionable speeds every single day. LOL! Quote: Originally Posted by pratik1992 I've also had a scare where I pressed the accelerator instead of the brake once. The way it lurches. Took me a few mins to calm down. But apart from that, it's just addictive! Exactly. Folks who drive the performance will know the feeling. I drove the regular Model 3 and that doesn’t give a similar kick. Quote: Originally Posted by pratik1992 Aaah. Fast turns are so fun in this car! Friend has a Mustang with performance upgrades, which used to feel amazing, and now feels like a truck!  And I love observing cars that change lanes like that on roadtrips. Model S looks great. BMW 5 series in black, I don't know what is it about that car, but it looks phenomenal in such scenarios. Oh yeah, muscle cars stand no chance with a Tesla. By the time they shift into gear, the Tesla is long gone. BMW sedans in black and especially souped up ones are a pleasure to watch on the freeways. And, they are usually going really fast. This is stupid but, I will tell you something. Call it BMW owners immaturity. But, I noticed here in US, BMW cars when they see another BMW alongside, suddenly their hormones go into an overdrive and an uninitiated race begins. Haha! I experienced that as recent as last week when an BMW M8 thought I was racing with him ( I was just at my own clipping speed) but, at the end I think he was surprised to see me keep up with him. If you haven’t seen my BMW ownership thread. Here it is : https://www.team-bhp.com/forum/test-...ml#post4990969 Quote: Originally Posted by pratik1992 There's one corner on a specific road where my car always phantom brakes, with not a soul in sight. I don't know why. Otherwise it's mostly fine.  But it is way too scared of cars that come in it's at a 90 degree angle (thinking a car turning in front of you from the opposite side). It freaks out and slams on the brakes way too early. I just disengage the autopilot proactively when I see something like that about to happen. Interesting to know about that one spot where you experience phantom braking always. You should probably report to Tesla and give the LAT LONG of that place. Maybe they will look into it. Quote: Originally Posted by pratik1992 I go by the same principle. I don't think I've ever managed 300+ on a charge. More like 250. But I do think driven sensibly, it can very well reach those numbers. Honestly, where I live the electricity is super cheap ( Night : 0.08/kwh, Day : 0.10/kwh) so I don’t really car about the range. But, I am generally curious and like to know how much average I’m getting. I do that religiously in my BMW as well (via. fuel consumption tracking app). Quote: Originally Posted by pratik1992 I had a puncture in the first month! But has been fine since then. Agree to summer/fall performance. I experienced that always in India. I buy a brand new car and 1st month there is a punter. Don’t know what’s the spookiness about that. Never understood. Interesting anecdote : I have owned 4 Brand new cars in US and have combined clocked about 150,000 miles (240,000kms) including the rental cars that I drive regularly for work related travel. Not a single puncture till date for any of the cars that I owned or driven till date (including rental cars) TOUCHWOOD. Quote: Originally Posted by pratik1992 But I am surprised that you don't have winter/all-seasons for the colder months? This is my first car in the US and hence the first cold season with one. I live in NJ, where it doesn't snow as much, but definitely gets pretty cold. Is almost under freezing temps for 2 months. And from all that I have read, summer tires absolutely useless and turn to hard plastics and aren't recommended under 45F. I know Princeton pretty well. I have been there multiple time on work and pleasure. A close pal lives in Princeton (incidentally he too drives a black Tesla LR)- Pic below of his car which I drive whenever I am in Princeton. NJ is not too snowy but, as you rightly said it gets pretty cold. You need to be super careful and keep a lot of distance between cars when driving in those conditions. I have not changed or plan to change to other set of tires because, whenever I go into the mountains or drive in snow conditions (we get pretty decent amount here in winters and we have a lot of mountains which is snow capped most part of the year), I take my Blau Rakete to tame those terrains. Quote: Originally Posted by pratik1992 I am contemplating getting a set. Just confused if I should get a set of smaller wheel+tire (18-19s) combo. Or mount new tires on the 20 Uberturbines. Also should I get winters or all seasons!?  Mounting winters would mean no road trip to the warmer states until remount the summers in March/April, else I'll wear those winters off like wax. All seasons would mean no trips up north in winter, and I really wanted to go to Quebec for Christmas. Decisions, decisions! I would recommend exact same wheel size tires for winter tires. Michelin Cross-climate 2 is really popular for light snowy conditions. Check them out. Harsh snow, its the Michelin X that works better. Quote: Originally Posted by pratik1992 Have only ever taken test rides of BMWs and other German cars. Always found them quite hard. Model 3's are great. Can vouch for them. ~500 miles to Toronto in about 9 hours. Was the least tired of any cars that I've driven that kind of distance. Nice. There was a time, I used to drive from Detroit to Boston ( 850 miles in 11-hours including stops) every quarter in my Mazda CX-9 and BMW and it used to be a breeze. Although, I haven’t done any long drives in Tesla yet (longest being 300 miles roundtrip), I am sure those long distance drives will be like a ride on a sofa. Quote: Originally Posted by pratik1992 Here's my mixed usage report. Have charged it in variety of places. I live in an apartment, so no home charging for me. Home in the image represent's the town's L2 charger (200V-30A) near my apartment. Work has L2 chargers ranging from  32A to 48A (210V). 48's have charged my car from 3% to 98% in 6-8 hours, I think. Superchargers on road trips. And the others being shopping malls. Ah! You live in an apartment. I understand now. No wonder you cost is higher. You need to be more dependent on superchargers than home or lower capacity charging equipment. My suggestion is to use superchargers as less as possible as it will have an impact on the battery longevity in the long run. Maximize usage of L2 chargers over Superchargers. Quote: Originally Posted by pratik1992 The best I have ever used in ANY car. Bear in mind, that I haven't driven many fancy cars with LASER and other super large matrix headlamps (Audi comes to mind). Absolutely love these lights at night. Converts the night into day. Haha! Quote: Originally Posted by pratik1992 I initially bought car thinking I'll keep it until just before the end of warranty. Probably replace it with another M3P, but with latest updates. Having seen the no-stalks Highland model, I might keep this longer. But then an important thing that's changing is the upcoming NACS port on pretty much all other American brands. If BMW/Porsche also start using NACS and Tesla superchargers too, I might consider them as well when the time comes. Well, the cost will also play a factor. Even I want the stalks, otherwise I will have nothing to fiddle with when driving. In anywise, I miss resting my hand on a gear shifter like in the BMW. I think, I will own this car till it will last which is a first for me in my car ownerships. Just love this electric beast !!!

source_target: 2023 Tesla Model 3 Performance (M3P) | 29,000 miles (47,000 kms) | Ownership Report. EDIT: Sold - Page 7
text: I've driven a couple of Tesla cars and have used them as Uber several times. I never cared to check out the sound system. This week I realized it was a big miss from me. My Uber driver played "When something is wrong with my baby" loudly  in his Model Y and I've never heard something so loud with clarity  and punch inside a car. We do tend to trash Tesla for build quality but there wasn't a single rattle. Impressed. I wanted to know more about Tesla's sound system and landed here. Quote: Originally Posted by mobike008 Tesla Model 3 Performance- Music System Review It immediately left an impression on me as the music quality was superior than any other car that I ever heard till date. Then I started doing some research on Tesla’s music system which strangely is not marketed at all by them as one of the stand-out feature in a Tesla. It’s a known fact that Tesla has hired Bang & Olufsen engineers and there is a claim that these are “unbranded” B&O system. I have personally sat in or drove several high-end cars (Mercedes, Porsche and BMW’s). This is a system is superlative compared to any of those systems in those cars. Good review. I agree and I can relate to this. I also don't understand why the truly premium sound system isn't marketed well. From a review . The sound system is absolutely next level. I revealed a while back  from a source within Tesla the brand had hired former Bang and Olufsen  engineers to develop a bespoke sound system. They came up with an  unbranded 13-speaker system that includes a subwoofer and two  amplifiers. The benefit of sourcing their own speakers and not showing a brand is  there are no expensive licensing fees to pay and you don’t need to work  within the narrow specifications of the particular audio brand. In my opinion it’s the best sound system on the market – and we’ve  tested some ultra-luxury brands with expensive high-end sound systems in  the past.

Some examples : Smooth Criminal- Michael Jackson Wicked Games- Chris Issac Gin & Tonic- Snoop Dogg (This song will make you gape- When the drink is poured in the glass at beginning of the song- It literally feels as if it's happening inside the car)..Lol! I revealed a while back  from a source within Tesla the brand had hired former Bang and Olufsen  engineers to develop a bespoke sound system. They came up with an  unbranded 13-speaker system that includes a subwoofer and two  amplifiers. The benefit of sourcing their own speakers and not showing a brand is  there are no expensive licensing fees to pay and you don’t need to work  within the narrow specifications of the particular audio brand. In my opinion it’s the best sound system on the market – and we’ve  tested some ultra-luxury brands with expensive high-end sound systems in  the past. Even I was surprised as never see any mention of Tesla ICE anywhere except on the Tesla forums. But, then I realize 8/10 people are not critical of what they own or have a taste to understand the nuances of it. For folks like us every small detail in an automobile matters. But, in the Tesla fraternity (forums, friends who own it), everyone swears by the premium sound system (not available in base models) If you were impressed with sound (songs) . Then you have not even experienced the full shebang. Btw, the reviews also mentioned that it sounds even more tight and better in a Model 3 vs. Model Y as there is less space for the sound waves to travel inside the car. Just listen to this video in a Tesla premium sound system in high volume. I will guarantee it will blow you away. Judge banging the award idol on the desk will reverberate inside the car. It’s surreal. https://youtu.be/x30k7GDTD0Q?si=m5tUEeyIOEZdum2N Tesla’s system is even more impressive when you are watching “video content”. It’s literally like sitting in an IMAX theatre with sound bouncing around. This is when the true experience of this amazing system will show its prowess. Cheers!
