source_target: December 2025: Electric Car Sales Figures & Analysis
text: Good to see the overall mass market dominance of Tata Motors ,M&M, and JSW MG. On the Bangalore roads, most visible EVs are from the Tata Motors at the top, MG at the second, followed by M&M, in that order.  Visibly, MG seems to close in on the gap with Tata, and perhaps they might win there with a few more choices in hand. It would be good if there are many more contenders doing upwards of 4K vehicles a month, which would implicitly mean many more mass market choices. However practically, I don't anticipate that will happen anytime soon, as a common factor among top 3 in the list is their dominating understanding of Indian market EVs at this time.   Everyone else is at a huge distance from these 3 folks. e-Vitara is going to be an outlier and an interesting watch, however that's just one EV that Maruti is bringing now. They truly should have EVs across their vehicle ranges to stir up the dominance of the trio. If for any company, Maruti Suzuki is the one with a fair chance to re-draw the charts of mass market EVs.

source_target: December 2025: Electric Car Sales Figures & Analysis
text: Maruti has had many a failed launch of the E Vitara. What is selling the vehicle? Repairability? This is their first EV if I'm not mistaken. They have zero expertise in said protocol. Just imagine there's a thermal runaway in a parked accidental car. Do they have the wherewithal on how to handle a metal fire in the middle of the night? I don't see how they price it better than say the fully loaded vinfast VF6 or a very close to excellent BE 6 pack 1 above (considering discounts) or a 9S pack 1 above (base model) in a pay-for-your-batterypack-as-needed model pyramid. Heck, the 9S is an absolute segment killer for someone who wants a well rounded EV in the 16-22L range. A prospect on the fence with 16L in their pocket will be swayed easily to look at the 9S. It's *right there* and within reach. Big car = sales. Offers 60kW to boot. Drive gently and get 450 kms range. I'm currently achieving 550+ in Delhi in the winters with the BE6. And someone with 22L to spare can look at the amazing pack 2 above or settle for the pack 1 above, 79kA. Now, I'm not a Mahindra shill. I abhor the bonnet tubelight and perceived gap. I'll attach a picture as proof of my distaste for the car, but it will sell, and we'll be looking at JSW dropping a position on this list, for sure. Meanwhile, Hyundai is headless. With the success of Indian homegrown products (which really, the harrier has shocked me with its sales numbers), there is no place left for the Ionic. The same can be said for BYD. Indians, given half a choice, to spend less and get more, will buy an established local brand's products. P. S. I see the 9S landing up in the taxi segment big time. That'll skew numbers, quick.

source_target: December 2025: Electric Car Sales Figures & Analysis
text: The EV market has its oddities. Since EVs deliver best value in cities, you’d expect entry level ones like Tiago or Punch to bring in the volumes but the volumes are in the higher segments. I guess this comes down to the following reasons: The larger EVs like Harrier and the Mahindra twins deliver superior price to performance over their ICE counterparts given the taxation norms. Buyers in this segment have higher risk appetite for EV technology The hatchback segment is shrinking as an overall trend and an EV in this segment does not offer better price to performance over ICE given the price sensitivity

source_target: Toyota Mirai Hydrogen fuel cell EV pilot study begins in India
text: Nitin Gadkari, Minister for Road Transport & Highways, has launched a pilot study on the Toyota Mirai Hydrogen Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle (FCEV) in India. Toyota Kirloskar Motor will be conducting the study in collaboration with the International Centre for Automotive Technology (ICAT). The aim is to evaluate the feasibility of the hydrogen-powered Mirai on Indian roads and climatic conditions. The Mirai is powered by a fuel cell that combines hydrogen and oxygen to produce electricity. The only tailpipe emission from a hydrogen FCEV is water. According to Gadkari, green hydrogen can be generated from renewable energy and biomass. This technology is said to have the potential to secure a clean and affordable energy future for India. Link to Team-BHP News

Mercedes-Benz CLA Electric bookings open in India Mercedes-Benz has opened bookings for the CLA Electric in India. The electric sedan will be available in two variants: CLA 200 ‘Standard Range’ and CLA 250+ ‘Long Range’, priced at Rs 55 lakh and Rs 59 lakh (tentative ex-showroom price). Customers can book the car with a token amount of Rs 1.5 lakh. Deliveries will commence from the end of April, starting with the CLA 250+ variant. CLA 200 deliveries will begin in June. The CLA Electric is based on the Mercedes-Benz Modular Architecture (MMA) and uses an 800-volt electric architecture. The CLA 250+ variant comes with an 85 kWh battery, offering a range of up to 792 km, whereas the CLA 200 has a claimed range of 542 km. Both variants of the CLA Electric will be offered with the same equipment level. The CLA 200 will have the ‘Progressive Line’ styling, featuring comfort seats, a multifunction sports leather steering wheel, and two trim options. Customers who pre-book the car will receive a complimentary Wall Box charger. Link to Team-BHP News

CLA has grown in dimensions nearly equal to C class. The dimensions of CLA are: 4,723 mm in length × 1,855 mm width × 1,468 mm height. BMW 330i's dimensions are: 4709 mm length x 1827 mm width x 1435 mm height. C class dimensions are: 4751 mm length x 1820 mm width x 1437 mm height. Additionally, CLA's ev kerb weight is 2055 kg and 330i's kerb weight is 1660 kg, which explains why it is a sec slower to the ton. CLA EV power to weight is 130.41 bhp/ton CLA EV torque to weight is 163 nm/ton 330i power to weight is 153 bhp/ton 330i torque to weight is 241 nm/ton Considering CLA EV has a reduction of 23 bhp and 78 nm, it is only 1 sec slower than 330i due to its instant EV power.

source_target: Is it OK to *only* fast-charge your electric car? No slow charging at all - Page 2
text: I’m with GTO on this one. The battery charge saga is the new petrol head non discussion replacing the  what oil discussion in ICE. In both cases, the reliability of either a battery or ICE (properly maintained) is always going to outlast its first owner without any issues. Exceptions for those who keep their EV or ICE car for 200K. It simply a non issue. Just like engine oil. As long as you stick to the spec you are never ever going to reap the benefits of it unless you keep the car for 200K km. And most EVs and ICE will still do well, albeit maybe with a little loss of performance . Jeroen

source_target: Is it OK to *only* fast-charge your electric car? No slow charging at all - Page 2

That means you should be able to install a 16A plug in your car park and no one can stop you. Perhaps it may be too long cabling that is the spoilsport? Maybe you can swap your parking with a resident who can have the plug installed in their parking lot. RWA is OK with me installing the point. But the long wiring, poorly done original wiring plan, low sanctioned load etc are the main reasons. Dont want to take a risk of adding a heavy load to the old wiring.

And most EVs and ICE will still do well, albeit maybe with a little loss of performance . Jeroen Hi Jeroen & GTO, can we assume that just like old days of smartphones that come with 5W chargers and then charging power increased to 18W, then 25W, then 33W and now it has reached to 100W & above by most manufacturers (except Apple & Samsung), EV charging has reached to that maturity level that fast charging of EV will not impact much on the EV battery in 5 to 8 years.  The only impact we can safely assume is slightly lower range after say 200 to 300 charging cycles. Hope most manufacturers BMS is efficient enough to see how much fast battery charging is good at that particular weather & charging speed so that fast charging do not impact battery health.  For example, if someone is using 100kW fast charger but outside temperature is 42 degree, BMS efficiently reduce the charging speed to say 25kW after some time so that battery health remain good & battery don't explode due to over heating.

source_target: Is it OK to *only* fast-charge your electric car? No slow charging at all - Page 2
text: I used to work with a team working on BMS for Lithium batteries (for phones), some of them chemistry PhDs. My understanding, some of it based on discussions. 1. The so called "fast" charging is not an issue for EVs, even on a regular basis. 2. It is not bad for the EV battery to charge as such to 100pct or even discharge below say 10pct. These levels are still OK as long as the the charge level does not stay at that level for too long. What that means is it is OK to charge your battery to 100pct if you are starting on a long trip (right before). It is also OK to let it discharge to 5pct as long as you are about to charge it on a charger in between. Dont leave it there at 100pct or below 5pct for long. That's what causes the damage. 3. The corollary of above is one should not charge  the EV to 100pct at home charger for daily commute if you don't really need to use that during the day. Hence the advisory to stay between 20-80 pct 4. Avoid huge temperature variations, if possible. But we don't have those sub-zero temperature winters mostly and can't avoid summers anyways. AC or DC fast hardly matters much, even on regular basis.

source_target: Is it OK to *only* fast-charge your electric car? No slow charging at all - Page 2

So do check with the car companies. Having said that there are a set of fleet-operated electric cars. These fleet electric cars survive just on fast charging.

Did you have OEM installers inspect ?

source_target: Is it OK to *only* fast-charge your electric car? No slow charging at all - Page 2
text: Quote: Originally Posted by GS300 RWA is OK with me installing the point. But the long wiring, poorly done original wiring plan, low sanctioned load etc are the main reasons. Dont want to take a risk of adding a heavy load to the old wiring. It's not a heavy load. The ZS EV's portable charger only pulls 8-9A. Something a household 15A socket can easily pull. If you are in Bangalore, I am happy to refer a competent electrician as well. Sanctioned load increases are also smooth nowadays via Sakala. You just need spare load of 2 kW while charging in the night. Get a competent electrician and use minimum 4 sq mm wire to install a plug point. You have nothing to worry about. I have such a point installed and it's more than enough Competent LFP EVs like the ZS EV can take continuous DC charging but like in computers, value of machine time is much lower than human time. In this case you are taking away an EV's biggest advantage of fueling up at home and turning it into a disadvantage by wasting time at a DC charger. There are Shoffr BYDs which have never been AC charged, so I think you should be fine but I will definitely not recommend this for the reasons mentioned above.

source_target: Is it OK to *only* fast-charge your electric car? No slow charging at all - Page 2

If you are in Bangalore, I am happy to refer a competent electrician as well. Sanctioned load increases are also smooth nowadays via Sakala. You just need spare load of 2 kW while charging in the night. These are great inputs, thank you! if the electrician you know is ready to visit my place in HSR, kindly refer him to me via pvt message. And I did not know that load enhancement is easy - last time I checked with one of those 'intermediary people' they asked me not to even explore that - saying it is so complicated that it is not worth it.

source_target: Is it OK to *only* fast-charge your electric car? No slow charging at all - Page 2
text: Quote: Originally Posted by GS300 These are great inputs, thank you! if the electrician you know is ready to visit my place in HSR, kindly refer him to me via pvt message. And I did not know that load enhancement is easy - last time I checked with one of those 'intermediary people' they asked me not to even explore that - saying it is so complicated that it is not worth it. Put a 16A socket not the dual use 5A/16A socket. As explained below. Also use a MCB which can dispate more heat than a switch. Quote: Originally Posted by shyampsunder It's not a heavy load. The ZS EV's portable charger only pulls 8-9A. Something a household 15A socket can easily pull. If you are in Bangalore, I am happy to refer a competent electrician as well. Sanctioned load increases are also smooth nowadays via Sakala. You just need spare load of 2 kW while charging in the night. The ZS EV pulls 12A easily on the AC side. If it's pulling only 8A to 9A then something is wrong in your installation or there is a bottleneck. The MG app can only show the DC current which will be lower than the actual AC current being pulled from the AC socket. If you use a 5/16A socket, the contact patch will be small. This causes excessive heat build up in the socket and the portable chargers plug will detect the heat and throttle down the current. But when you use a 16A socket that fits very very tight, the contact area is more and the socket will be cooler, so the portable charger increases the amperage draw.

source_target: Is it OK to *only* fast-charge your electric car? No slow charging at all - Page 2
text: Quote: Originally Posted by DIY410 The ZS EV pulls 12A easily on the AC side. If it's pulling only 8A to 9A then something is wrong in your installation or there is a bottleneck. The MG app can only show the DC current which will be lower than the actual AC current being pulled from the AC socket. If you use a 5/16A socket, the contact patch will be small. This causes excessive heat build up in the socket and the portable chargers plug will detect the heat and throttle down the current. I am using the Legrand Tata Motors box which comes with the dedicated 16A socket. It has no such heat build up and I know the app only shows DC input. That is 5A at 400V. The charger does go upto 6A at 400V which is 11A at 220V but that is super rare and this is the observation across multiple ZS EV owners. Only Tata EV's portable chargers regularly pull above 12A. MG's portable unit is very conservative which I appreciate.

source_target: Is it OK to *only* fast-charge your electric car? No slow charging at all - Page 2
text: Hello all. Its been 2.5 years since I bought home a Nexon EV Max. It Has clocked nearly 25k Kms on the odo while being predominantly being driven within city limits. I got it with a 3.3 Kw slow charger that came with the vehicle and was installed at home as per TAMO specifications. I have always been slow charging the car (overnight) at home since then. I keep a cut off close to 15-20% minimum SOC and always charge it to 100 %. I think I've just used 50 Kw fast charging only twice in it's entire life span so far. Iam happy to share that , till date (touchwood) haven't faced any BMS/HV critical errors or any sort of mishaps whatsoever. I'm consistently getting a 225-250 kms range on a 15-20% SOC on all conditions without any issues. Been driving it 60-70% on ECO mode and the rest in city mode all throughout. Barring minor TAMO service issues aside, have to say that my vehicle lived up to expectations and continues to deliver on a day to day basis.

source_target: Is it OK to *only* fast-charge your electric car? No slow charging at all - Page 2
text: I am now utterly and thoroughly confused. You must not do DC fast charging all the time. In fact, at least 80% of the time, charging must be on slow charge No, it doesn't matter if you fast charge or slow charge. After all,  fleet taxis and buses are fast charging all the time. Won't matter for 7-8 yrs or 200K kms. NMC batteries can be fast charged all the time; LFP batteries have to be slow charged mostly. No, NMC or LFP makes no difference to the fast vs slow charging debate You should not charge the battery upto 100% all the time. 80% is optimal. You should slow charge the battery to 100% often, for cell balancing You should not let the SOC go below 10% Once in x months, you HAVE to take it below 10% Look at your car's manual and do what it says. My car (ZSEV)'s manual, in 224 pages of blah blah has one small paragraph that says use slow charging as much as possible and that going up to 100% is the only way to reach the optimum equilibrium state (equalisation charge). My head swims. Whenever I browse for authoritative guidance on best practice for battery management, I get contradictory information. Being severely challenged electrical engineering wise, I have no clue; I am just surprised that there isn't a definitive & clear scientific answer for dumbos like me to blindly follow. I was told by the MG service centre at the 5000 kms service check that the battery SOH had degraded by 2%. When my jaw hit the floor, the advisor says this is "common" to all cars and mine was nothing "unusual". When I charge upto 100%, my range still shows 400 kms. I have reconciled to doing whatever is practically easiest in charging. That's it. To hell with the consequences !

source_target: Is it OK to *only* fast-charge your electric car? No slow charging at all - Page 2
text: Quote: Originally Posted by Sahil However, the latter confuses me because if I charge only to 80% I will be charging more often compared to doing it to 100% which means more cycles of charge that is also a deterrent to battery life. There are several theories out that, not sure which are essential and which are not. Upto 80 percent is not a full charge cycle like 0-100. Many people abroad have been using the free DC charging they get as their only/main charging with no noticeable battery degradation.

source_target: Is it OK to *only* fast-charge your electric car? No slow charging at all - Page 2
text: Quote: Originally Posted by Secretariat I was told by the MG service centre at the 5000 kms service check that the battery SOH had degraded by 2%. When my jaw hit the floor, the advisor says this is "common" to all cars and mine was nothing "unusual". When I charge upto 100%, my range still shows 400 kms. I have reconciled to doing whatever is practically easiest in charging. That's it. To hell with the consequences ! The degradation you saw at service center is mostly not true. There is a time based degradation counter which goes down by 0.01% every day irrespective of whether you use the car or charge the car. This degradation is adjusted with real measurement of degradation typically when the car is charged from a low charge % to 100%. This real measurement happens more frequently after year one when SoH goes below 97%. This is also when the range displayed goes below 400 at 100%. I can go on and on about it but your conclusion is perfect. The LFP battery is very resilient, charge as you wish, doesn't matter AC or DC, doesn't matter if it's 10% - 100% or 20% - 100%. The car is a tool to give you convenience, don't waste time and energy on optimising for degradation etc.

source_target: Tesla Model Y launched in India at ₹59.89L - Page 4
text: Quote: Originally Posted by volkman10 > If duties are reduced to 15% under an Indo-US trade deal, there could be a saving of around 20-22 lakhs, with the Tesla Model Y prices coming down to 38-44 lakhs. Import Duties Drive Up Costs Tesla's Indian pricing structure reveals the challenge facing the company: Model Y RWD: ₹59.89 lakh (ex-showroom), approximately ₹61.07 lakh on-road Model Y Long Range RWD: ₹67.89 lakh (ex-showroom), approximately ₹69.15 lakh on-road Full Self-Driving (FSD) capability: Additional ₹6 lakh However, the pricing disparity with global markets is stark: United States: $44,990 (₹37.8 lakh approximately) China: 263,500 yuan (₹31.2 lakh approximately) Germany: 45,970 euros (₹46.1 lakh approximately) India: ₹59.89 lakh Import duties account for an estimated ₹25-30 lakhs of the final price – representing 40-45% of the final bill. India currently imposes 70-100% import duty on completely built-up vehicles, making Tesla one of the most expensive markets globally for the Model Y. ... How Much Could Model Y Prices Drop? If Indo-US trade negotiations succeed in reducing India's automotive import duties, Tesla buyers could see substantial savings. Current import duty impact is estimated at ₹25 lakh per vehicle. If duties are reduced to 15%, there could be a saving of around 20-22 lakhs, with the prices coming down to 38-44 lakhs. Such reductions could bring the Model Y prices, making the model significantly more competitive with existing premium EVs like Mahindra BE6 (priced around Rs 22-27 lakhs). ... The US-India trade agreement will apply to imports from the US, not imports from China.  I suspect EU, UK, and Japan will push India and get similar duty rates as the US.  But, India will not allow such low import duties for goods made in China. And with Tesla's Thailand car plant cancelled, there is no cheap manufacturing site to import from. So, these 22Lac price drop expectations are unrealistic. Prices can drop if Tesla allocates a small amount of capacity from the US plants to India, and this is feasible given dropping sales in the US and low volumes in India in the ₹50-70L price range.  But due to the higher costs in the US, the price drop will be hardly half.  And that drop means a risk of steep depreciation for early Indian buyers. Tesla may still do good bookings in the first few months against the Mercs and BMWs due to some pent-up demand, but volumes may drop to a trickle after that. Tesla is notorious for poor maintenance and repair service even in the US, with long delays for parts.  The first few stories of Tesla cars stuck in repairs for weeks will also stop potential buyers.

text: I feel the TESLA Model-Y pricing is very good. It covers a broad spectrum of cars ranging from 50L to 70L where ICE car prospects would want something new. 2 Segment of customers that might be low-hanging fruit for TESLA. -iX1 prospects are not that price sensitive, and won't mind spending 10L extra -Tiguan/Kodiaq I feel owning a TESLA is more of a Emotion than anything else. You also get bragging right to be among the first lot of owners. It's one of the rare car makers that really doesn't care about launching new generation models. Since the design is timeless, like a BMW 5 series (F10), or a G-wagon, the small tweaks in hardware and software enhancements keep the buyers happy. I feel this strategy somehow also makes the existing owners hop-on to their car for a long time, as they don't miss much from new generation of cars. I hope we see more models from TESLA soon.

source_target: Tesla Model Y launched in India at ₹59.89L - Page 4
text: Great to see Tesla enter India at long last, and glad they bit the bullet and came in with CBU pricing. While the 64 KWH and 79 KWH battery packs may seem small, Teslas have always been more efficient than peers. Assuming 15% better mileage than the Ioniq 5, you should get about 13.5 KWH/ 100 km or a real world range of 470 km on the RWD and 550 km on the LR. The car will come with CCS2 connectors and therefore can be used everywhere. And the boot space of the Model Y is extraordinary to say the least. Of course it is not going to be a volume player - may sell between 100 and 250 units a month - which justifies only CBU operation for a single product brand. And if I were choosing between this and the BYD, I would probably pick this - of course Elon is not going to take losses forever but geopolitical factors as seen by the recent moves at JSW tell you what is the risk you take with some other brands. But yes, I am not in the market for a car in the near future and a lot will depend on how Teslas improve as competition, especially the locals, catch up.

text: With the current war, the issue will be more with respect to the natural gas and not as much for petrol/diesel. Since the gas has many more use cases beyond transport, the overall demand cannot be substituted. The delay in getting the regular supplies also will have an indirect impact for lot of industries. I was just doing some basic research, for our domestic requirements, we can actually switch to a electric induction thereby reduce the dependence on the gas. In addition to counter balance the import of gas, it also is more efficient since electric induction stoves are about 80-90% efficient whereas the gas stoves are at about 50-60%. A simple move can actually make a big difference. There also ancillary benefits, ambient temperature in kitchen don't rise, the release pollutants are also controlled, due the efficiency and otherwise, the cost for cooking is also lower. Overall, worth considering though its easier said than done.

Kudos to Hyundai for bringing the first real usable EV. This really is a sincere and serious product unlike the crap dished out by Tata and Mahindra in the form of Tigor EV, eVerito, etc. The upcoming MG eZS is also a fantastic effort (from all the UK reviews and India previews). If I could make a case for another car (I really don't have a need for a 3rd car nor the time to look after a 3rd car), I would definitely go for Kona or eZS. I fully understand that these are still early days and both these cars are good for local city usage for now (eventually range will increase, charging times will decrease, etc.). However, I just have one technical question - if I am ~20km away from the nearest town/charging station, somewhere in the ghats of kokan or Uttarakhand and my Kona battery fully discharges (for whatever reason, say I ignored low charge warning or the charge went down non-linearly because battery is now 4 years old or something else), what are my options? If this happens with my current car, I will just wave at a truck, ask for a ride to next petrol pump, explain the situation and get some petrol in the bottle. But what can I do with a Kona or eZS that's fully discharged in the middle of nowhere? Even "dakka start" won't work, right? Disclaimer: This is a purely technical question, no pride or prejudice for/against EVs or IC engines

But what can I do with a Kona or eZS that's fully discharged in the middle of nowhere? Even "dakka start" won't work, right? The only real option is to call RSA or known flat-bed pick up service. Use Google or car maker's RSA helpline to find the nearest charging station. Get the car to that station & charge it. Will be a good costly affair overall & henceforth one won't forget to ensure the car is fully charged & batteries are upto the task of reaching point B when starting from A. On a lighter note, if one is really nervous, get a small diesel generator in the boot. Get (or make one as a DIY) suitable rating charger. And charge the car yourself for emergency . That would be pretty much like thumbing the nose to the "green" philosophy of an EV though.

You can tow the car to the nearest charging station and if the EV has a permanent magnet motor (model 3, kona, mg ezs) you can *probably* charge your battery too while on tow. In one youtube video a model 3 towed for 1 mile charges the battery with more than 1 mile worth of juice. Of course this will be at the expense of towing vehicle fuel consumption and load up its engine. Loads of EV car videos available on the youtube by early EV adopters mostly tesla users and many of these questions we have are answered there. In another video tesla model S (induction motor so no regen when battery at 0) is purposely run down to 0 battery and it gets towed by another tesla to a supercharger. Same can be done by Like an ICE car there will be no power steering and possibly no brake servo either, but the EV would be heavier when compared to a similarly sized conventional vehicle and literally heavy to handle. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IPDxt02V0vo https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sswtRgTd7wg

Just concluded our Mahabalipuram trip with a total mileage of around 720 Kms with my Kona EV. Here are the stats, its all self explanatory. The resort we stayed - Radisson Blu, was very accommodative when we requested for a charging point Onward trip Started with 338 km / 99% SOC Climate ON @ 21c Cruise @ 70 kmph Level 0 recuperation Drive mode: Eco Reached Mahabalipuram: 351.9 Km / 26% SOC Average EV consumption: 12.4 km/kWh Return trip Started with 348 km / 100% SOC Climate ON @ 21c Cruise @ 70 kmph Level 0 recuperation Drive mode: Eco Reached Home(Bangalore): 351.7 km / 4% SOC Average EV consumption: 9.4 km/kWh The EV consumption was particularly high during my return trip due to higher levels of inclination, as Bangalore is about 920 meters from sea level

In your experience, how much would the range have differed if you had driven at say 100kmph?

Quote: Originally Posted by speedmiester Great stats. Any reason why you didn't use regeneration? This should have extended your range. How is the comfort? I felt the Kona suspension is on the stiffer side. There is no point in using regen on open roads such as highways. The best situation for any vehicle is to tame that coasting speed as much as possible. Regen if used for breaking then that absolutely makes sense. I often use the left regen pedal to pull and hold, that allows it to generate a higher force while decelerating, that helps increase the range. I have noticed the normal break pedal also participates in the regen process along with the 3 available regen modes. Of course the later is for a more relaxed driving style, suited especially to city driving conditions but the former also allows for regen whenever you want to decelerate. With these many options for regen, the best and the most effective way to recuperate kinetic energy during breaking is to use the break pedal itself, and and at times the left - "1 pedal driving" feature. Quote: Originally Posted by Shreyans_Jain Doesn’t having cruise set at 70 get bothersome on long trips? Especially in an electric car with all the silence? Seems to be a sure shot recipe for drowsiness, and of course you’d be overtaken by everyone left right and center. In your experience, how much would the range have differed if you had driven at say 100kmph? I absolutely agree but I haven`t been on a long trip with my Kona EV until yesterday. I was very skeptical as there are no EV charging infra available in my chosen route, or for any national highways for that matter. Having said that, its important to understand the limitations of an EV on a graded basis. How well you actually know its going to perform, unless you take the road less traveled, makes sense when you have maximum options to consider for any untoward situations or scenarios. I feel driving at a higher speed shouldn`t tremendously affect range, you`ll have to let me experience it though, before i can comment, i`m afraid

Quote: Originally Posted by pkirwadi Onward trip Started with 338 km / 99% SOC Reached Mahabalipuram: 351.9 Km / 26% SOC Average EV consumption: 12.4 km/kWh Return trip Started with 348 km / 100% SOC Reached Home(Bangalore): 351.7 km / 4% SOC Average EV consumption: 9.4 km/kWh So, for your total trip of ~700 kms, average consumption is 10.9 km/kWh. Based on highest slab (Rs 7.8 per unit) in Karnataka electricity tariff, its Rs 0.72 per km. For 700 kms, cost is Rs 500 . Most likely you might have paid more toll fee than fuel bill for the trip . Even if you had traveled at Rs 100 kmph, it would have cost less than Rs 1 per km and Rs 700 per the trip. I understand that you wanted to play safe as the charging options are less. In comparison, Creta diesel fuel bill would be Rs 2,380 assuming 20 kmpl mileage for 700 km trip. Creta petrol fuel would be Rs 3,300 assuming 16 kmpl mileage. It will be interesting to see the real world efficiency comparisons in future between Kona, MG eZS, nexon, etc... Among the traditional companies, Hydunai/Kia EVs have the best efficiency numbers competing closely with Teslas.

I was very skeptical as there are no EV charging infra available in my chosen route, or for any national highways for that matter. Having said that, its important to understand the limitations of an EV on a graded basis. How well you actually know its going to perform, unless you take the road less traveled, makes sense when you have maximum options to consider for any untoward situations or scenarios. I feel driving at a higher speed shouldn`t tremendously affect range, you`ll have to let me experience it though, before i can comment, i`m afraid I am pretty much inclined ,for an Ev in coming future and eKona is on top of the list. Have three queries : 1. Can you update exactly how much units of electricity it consumes to charge via the conventional charging i.e three pin charger which is given along. 2. With the Home Dc charger also how much units of electricity are consumed for about 80-90% of charging 3. Dc Fast charging cost with charging stations 🚉 Actually,I am planning myself a Solar installation for charging whichever Ev i purchase in near future ,but I don’t want a coal operated electricity. I already have electric bicycles for myself ,so pretty much inclined for an four wheeler Ev. Would appreciate the information Regards

Calculating electricity units for charge is pretty easy. the Kona has 39.2Kwh battery so it is 39.2 Units under ideal conditions, since we don't get those, you can take 80% efficiency in charging and we can get around 39.2/0.8 = 49 Units. for your second query, there is no Home DC charger, it's Home AC Fast charger, which pumps out 7.2KW per hour rather than then 2KW you get from a normal plug, the units consumed will be same. Fortum has installed few fast chargers in Delhi, it pumps in 50KW and you can get your car to 80% pretty rapidly, the charges are Rs50 + (14.99/min to 18.99/min - based on charger location). So let's say you are charge for 50 minutes which is enough for 80% for Kona, then you will end up with a bill of 50 + (750 - 950) which is way lesser than what you would pay for fossil fuel.

source_target: Tesla India gets around 600 orders since launch | Falls short of its own expectations - Page 2
text: A newsreport today in Economic Times reaffirms the Tesla struggle to stay afloat in India. They have 100 cars waiting to find new buyers since four months, even in India's commercial capital. It means that if someone buys the car now or even upto a few months from now, he gets a car that was manufactured in 2025. Many of those who booked the Model Y SUV, have backed out having cancelled their bookings. Certain variants of the SUV in question are to be sold at discounts of about Rs 2 lakhs. We were expecting news about new and potentially possible Tesla showrooms in other metros. But the grim sales scenario, in the backdrop of several months of pre-launch lobbying by Elon Musk and Co. to derive duty concessions and fast-track files in the corridors of power in Delhi have not paid dividends to the Tesla management. It exposes the utter failure of Tesla's marketing intelligence inputs from India. Cars priced in the same brackets where Teslas are slotted sell too well, especially the ICE models by luxury car makers. Hence, pricing is no constraint but there are other factors Tesla needs to investigate, lest such dampening sales compels Tesla to do either a Harley or a Chevrolet or a Ford like exit sooner or later. According to the newsreport BMW's EViX1 and BYD's Sealion 7 EV's have lower price tags and are preferred by buyers. Quote: Tesla Inc. is struggling to offload about a third of the initial vehicles it imported to India last year, as some prospective buyers who made early bookings have backed out, according to people familiar with the matter. Sluggish demand has left roughly 100 vehicles in search of buyers four months after the Elon Musk-led company shipped in a batch of about 300 Model Y sport utility vehicles, said the people, who didn’t want to identified because the information isn’t public. To clear out unsold stock, Tesla is offering discounts of as much as Rs 2,00,000 ($2,200) on certain variants of the SUV, they said. It’s an early sign of challenges as Tesla attempts to crack the world’s third-largest passenger-vehicle market with one of its cheaper entry-level models. The carmaker, which debuted in India in July, was relying on its brand power to drive sales despite local levies touching 110% for imported cars. The Economic Times news:- https://m.economictimes.com/industry.../126536500.cms
